proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic Operator Network Framework for Stabilized Isogeometric Analysis of Wave Propagation in Acoustics and Electromagnetics
proposals.motivation: High-frequency wave propagation and electromagnetic field simulations are critical for applications like antenna design, photonics, and noise control, but suffer from numerical dispersion and pollution errors in standard finite element methods. The Variational Multiscale (VMS) framework has proven highly effective for stabilizing fluid dynamics problems, but its systematic application to time-harmonic wave equations using high-order, smooth Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) basis functions remains underdeveloped, particularly for complex geometries handled by trimmed NURBS.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a VMS framework, implemented through the architecture of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VMONs), will provide a robust, parameter-free stabilization for IGA discretizations of the Helmholtz and Maxwell equations, significantly reducing pollution errors on coarse, locally refined trimmed meshes.
proposals.method: We will extend the VMON architecture, which embeds variational structure into neural networks, to learn optimal fine-scale models for wave operators. The method will be integrated within an IGA framework, leveraging Hughes' expertise in splines and trimmed mesh analysis. Specifically, we will: 1) Derive the VMS formulation for the time-harmonic scalar Helmholtz and vector Maxwell's equations using IGA basis functions. 2) Design a VMON to approximate the fine-scale Green's operator, trained using fine-scale solutions from resolved problems on representative domain patches. 3) Implement the coarse-scale stabilized system within a solver supporting locally refined quadrilateral layouts, computing accurate reaction forces and fluxes on trimmed domains.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, stabilized IGA formulation for wave problems that is inherently stable across a wide frequency range. It will provide a concrete bridge between the VMS philosophy and operator network machine learning for computational wave physics. The resulting method will enable more efficient simulation of wave phenomena on complex CAD geometries without manual stabilization tuning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the VMS framework for wave problems with the VMON architecture and its implementation within a practical, trimmed IGA setting, creating an intelligent, learned stabilization that respects the underlying variational structure of the wave operators.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic Framework for Stabilized Methods: Unification via the Fine-Scale Green's Function
proposals.motivation: Stabilized finite element methods, such as SUPG, GLS, and VMS, are essential for solving advection-dominated and multiscale problems, but they are often derived and analyzed in an ad-hoc manner. A unified, rigorous mathematical framework is needed to connect these methods, understand their stability and accuracy properties, and guide the development of new ones. This is particularly relevant for isogeometric analysis (IGA), where high-order smooth splines offer new opportunities for stabilized formulations.
proposals.hypothesis: The fine-scale Green's function, as formalized within the Variational Multiscale (VMS) framework, provides a canonical mathematical structure from which all major stabilized methods can be systematically derived. Furthermore, the concept of variational mimicry can be used to construct operator networks that approximate this Green's function, leading to optimally stabilized schemes.
proposals.method: We will employ the Variational Multiscale framework to formally express the fine-scale solution via a Green's function acting on the coarse-scale residual. Using this formalism, we will derive conditions under which specific approximations of the Green's function recover SUPG, GLS, and other methods. We will then leverage the 'Variationally Mimetic Operator Network' (VarMION) concept to design data-informed or analytically constructed approximations of the Green's function, ensuring stability and accuracy. The methods will be implemented and tested within an isogeometric analysis framework, utilizing trimmed NURBS and locally refined meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a unified theoretical foundation for stabilized finite element methods, clarifying their interrelationships and approximation properties. It will yield a new class of 'mimetic' stabilized methods with provable stability, directly applicable to challenging problems in computational fluid dynamics and transport. The framework will also bridge Hughes's prior work on VMS, isogeometric analysis, and the new VarMION concept.
proposals.novelty: While the fine-scale Green's function is known in VMS theory, its systematic use as a unifying tool to formally derive and connect all major stabilized methods is novel. Combining this with the concept of variational mimicry to create optimized stabilization operators represents a significant advance beyond current ad-hoc parameter selection.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for Stabilized Isogeometric Analysis of Non-Newtonian Fluids
proposals.motivation: Non-Newtonian fluid models, such as power-law or Carreau fluids, are critical for simulating biological flows, polymer processing, and geophysical phenomena. Standard finite element and isogeometric analysis (IGA) methods often suffer from numerical instabilities and poor mass conservation when applied to these nonlinear systems, especially on complex trimmed CAD geometries common in engineering. A robust, structure-preserving discretization framework is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Variationally Mimetic Operator Network (VarMiON) architecture, which learns stable discrete operators from data while preserving key variational structures, can be extended to provide a physics-informed stabilization mechanism for the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations with shear-dependent viscosity, overcoming the limitations of ad-hoc stabilization techniques.
proposals.method: We will leverage the theoretical foundation of the VarMiON, a neural operator designed to be consistent with the underlying variational formulation. The method will be integrated into an IGA framework using locally refined trimmed meshes. The VarMiON will be trained on high-fidelity solution data for canonical non-Newtonian flows to learn a stabilized discrete formulation that enforces inf-sup stability and local mass conservation. This learned, mimetic formulation will then be applied to solve flows on complex geometries, utilizing the quadrature and layout optimization techniques from Hughes's recent work on trimmed surfaces.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-aware stabilization scheme for non-Newtonian IGA, directly applicable to industrial CAD models. It will advance the field of structure-preserving neural operators by demonstrating their efficacy for a challenging class of nonlinear PDEs. The resulting framework will provide more accurate and stable predictions of reaction forces and fluxes in complex fluid-structure interaction problems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the data-driven, structure-preserving VarMiON framework with the geometric flexibility of trimmed IGA specifically for non-Newtonian fluids, moving beyond traditional penalty-based or residual-based stabilization methods. This creates a new paradigm for learning numerically stable discretizations tailored to nonlinear constitutive laws.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Stabilization for High-Fidelity Isogeometric Analysis of Compressible Navier-Stokes Flows
proposals.motivation: The accurate and stable finite element simulation of compressible Navier-Stokes equations, particularly for high-speed and turbulent flows, remains a significant challenge in computational fluid dynamics. Traditional stabilization techniques like SUPG can be problem-dependent and may lack a rigorous variational foundation, leading to spurious oscillations or excessive numerical dissipation that corrupts critical flow features. A robust, mathematically sound stabilization framework is essential for leveraging the high-order smoothness and geometric accuracy of Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) in demanding aerospace and energy applications.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the Variationally Mimetic Operator Network (VarMiON) framework, which constructs data-driven stabilization operators that preserve underlying variational structures, can be systematically extended to provide superior stability and accuracy for compressible flow problems compared to classical, hand-tuned methods.
proposals.method: We will extend the VarMiON architecture to accept inputs parameterizing the local compressible flow state (e.g., Mach number, density, velocity gradients) and the IGA discretization. The network will be trained to output a local stabilization operator that minimizes a loss function combining residual-based stabilization targets and mimetic constraints derived from the weak form of the compressible equations. This learned operator will be integrated into a stabilized IGA solver built upon the core Hughes group libraries for splines and trimmed NURBS geometries, with validation on benchmark problems including transonic airfoils and supersonic boundary layers.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-aware stabilization scheme specifically designed for compressible flows within an IGA setting, demonstrably reducing numerical artifacts. It will provide a generalizable template for embedding variational mimetic principles into data-assisted computational mechanics, moving beyond incompressible and linear model problems. The resulting open-source implementation will be a valuable tool for high-fidelity CFD.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the emerging VarMiON concept—a data-driven yet structure-preserving approach—with the complexities of compressible flow physics, which involves shock-capturing and strong coupling of equations, a combination not explored in pre-2026 literature. This moves beyond applying neural networks as black-box correctors to embedding them as architecture-preserving components within a well-established computational framework.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for High-Fidelity Simulation of 3D Unsteady Viscous Flows
proposals.motivation: Accurate and efficient simulation of complex 3D unsteady viscous flows, such as turbulent boundary layers or vortex-dominated aerodynamics, remains computationally prohibitive with traditional high-fidelity methods. The recent development of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiONs) offers a promising data-driven framework that respects underlying variational structure, but its performance has only been demonstrated on relatively simple benchmark problems. Validating this architecture on industrially relevant 3D flow physics is a critical next step for computational fluid dynamics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the VarMiON architecture, by embedding variational mimetic principles, can be extended and trained to accurately predict the evolution of complex 3D, unsteady, incompressible Navier-Stokes flows, achieving significant speed-up over conventional isogeometric analysis (IGA) solvers while preserving key physical invariants.
proposals.method: We will implement the VarMiON framework within an isogeometric analysis (IGA) codebase, leveraging B-spline and NURBS basis functions for geometric exactness. The network will be trained on high-fidelity simulation data generated using IGA solvers for canonical 3D flows (e.g., flow past a sphere, 3D lid-driven cavity). The input to the VarMiON will be the initial condition and time-dependent boundary data on a coarse mesh, and the output will be the predicted velocity and pressure fields. Training will enforce mimetic constraints on mass, momentum, and energy to ensure physical consistency.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first rigorous assessment of VarMiON performance for 3D unsteady viscous flows, providing benchmarks for accuracy and computational efficiency. It will establish a pipeline for integrating geometric modeling (via IGA), high-fidelity simulation, and physics-embedded machine learning, advancing the field of scientific machine learning for fluid mechanics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the fusion of Hughes's core expertise in isogeometric analysis and splines with the nascent VarMiON architecture, targeting a high-dimensional, nonlinear, time-dependent 3D problem where preserving mimetic structure is crucial for stability and physical realism, moving beyond proof-of-concept 2D or scalar problems.

proposals.title: A Priori and A Posteriori Error Analysis for Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks in Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: The recent introduction of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMION) offers a promising data-driven approach to constructing stable numerical schemes for partial differential equations, particularly within the isogeometric analysis (IGA) framework. However, rigorous convergence theory for such hybrid methods, which blend neural network architectures with variational principles, remains underdeveloped. Establishing robust error estimates is crucial for their reliable application to complex problems in computational mechanics and physics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the VarMION architecture, by construction, inherits sufficient variational structure to permit a rigorous mathematical analysis, yielding optimal a priori convergence rates and computable a posteriori error estimators that are reliable and efficient.
proposals.method: We will first perform an a priori error analysis by decomposing the total error into approximation error of the underlying IGA spline space and the operator approximation error of the neural network component, leveraging techniques from the numerical analysis of Petrov-Galerkin methods and approximation theory for neural networks. For the a posteriori analysis, we will construct residual-based error estimators that account for the VarMION's specific stabilized form, drawing on established methodologies for isogeometric methods and trimmed meshes. The theoretical results will be verified numerically on benchmark problems, including linear elasticity and advection-diffusion, using the open-source IGA library GeoPDEs.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first comprehensive convergence theory for a variationally mimetic neural operator, bridging a critical gap between data-driven methods and rigorous numerical analysis. It will deliver practical, computable error indicators to guide adaptive refinement for VarMION-enhanced IGA simulations, enhancing their reliability for industrial-scale problems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in extending classical finite element error analysis to a hybrid neural-network-based variational scheme, specifically targeting the unique architecture of VarMIONs. This analysis is non-trivial due to the non-linear parameterization of the discrete operators by the network.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks with Isogeometric Geometric Multigrid for Scalable Linear Solves
proposals.motivation: Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) and its stabilized formulations for complex geometries, including trimmed NURBS, generate high-fidelity linear systems that are computationally expensive to solve with direct methods. While geometric multigrid (GMG) is a theoretically optimal solver for IGA, its robust application to stabilized formulations on locally refined and trimmed meshes remains a significant challenge. Efficient solvers are critical for scaling high-order methods to practical engineering problems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the structure-preserving properties of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiON) with a tailored geometric multigrid framework will yield a robust, scalable, and parameter-robust solver for the linear systems arising from stabilized IGA formulations on trimmed, locally refined meshes.
proposals.method: We will develop an algebraic framework to construct prolongation and restriction operators that are consistent with the variational mimetic structure of the discrete problem, ensuring stability across multigrid levels. For geometric multigrid, we will leverage the inherent spline hierarchy of IGA, adapting smoothing operations to account for stabilization terms. For algebraic multigrid on severely trimmed layouts, we will use the equivalence class-based quadrilateral layouts to inform coarsening strategies. The solver will be integrated into the existing framework for computing reaction forces on trimmed meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a production-ready, scalable linear solver specifically designed for modern IGA with trimming and local refinement, significantly reducing solution time for large-scale problems. It will provide a concrete bridge between the theoretical efficiency of IGA multigrid and the practical complexities of stabilized formulations in computer-aided design (CAD)-integrated analysis.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the mimetic, structure-aware learning of VarMiON with the rigorous numerical analysis of multigrid for IGA, creating a solver whose construction is informed by the underlying variational structure of the stabilized problem, rather than treating the matrix as a generic algebraic entity.

proposals.title: A Time-Discontinuous Galerkin/Least-Squares Formulation with Isogeometric Spatial Discretization for Transient Advection-Diffusion Problems
proposals.motivation: The time-discontinuous Galerkin/Least-Squares (TDGLS) formulation is a powerful method for transient problems, offering high-order accuracy and stability in time. However, its spatial discretization is often limited to traditional low-order finite elements. Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) provides superior geometric exactness and higher-order, high-continuity basis functions, which have proven advantageous for spatial operators in steady-state problems. Integrating IGA's superior spatial approximation capabilities into the TDGLS framework promises a unified, high-fidelity space-time method for challenging transient physics.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that employing Non-Uniform Rational B-Spline (NURBS) basis functions from IGA for the spatial discretization within the TDGLS formulation will yield a method with enhanced accuracy per degree-of-freedom and improved stability for transient advection-diffusion problems, particularly on complex geometries, compared to standard finite element spatial discretization.
proposals.method: We will develop the variational formulation by combining the time-discontinuous Galerkin method with a least-squares stabilization term, using NURBS basis functions for the spatial trial and test functions. The implementation will leverage existing IGA libraries (e.g., PetIGA, G+Smo) for spatial integration and assembly. We will employ a predictor-multicorrector algorithm for time integration. The core technical challenge is the efficient evaluation of the TDGLS variational form on the IGA spatial mesh, ensuring proper handling of the temporal jump terms and the least-squares operator. Validation will be performed on benchmark problems with known analytical solutions, followed by application to problems with complex CAD-derived geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, high-order accurate space-time discretization scheme that tightly integrates CAD geometry with analysis. It will provide a rigorous numerical analysis of the combined method's stability and convergence properties. The resulting computational framework will be a significant step towards robust, high-fidelity simulation of transient phenomena in engineering design, such as fluid-structure interaction or thermal stress evolution.
proposals.novelty: While IGA has been combined with space-time methods like continuous Galerkin, its integration with the specifically time-discontinuous GLS formulation is unexplored. The novelty lies in leveraging the high-continuity of NURBS across spatial elements to enhance the accuracy of the least-squares stabilization operator, which typically involves higher-order spatial derivatives.

proposals.title: Adjoint-Based Inverse Design with Stabilized Isogeometric Formulations: A Variationally Mimetic Framework
proposals.motivation: Inverse design for complex PDE-constrained systems, such as nanoliposome drug delivery or varmion-based spintronics, requires robust and efficient gradient computation for shape and material optimization. The adjoint method is the standard for high-dimensional design spaces, but its rigorous derivation for stabilized finite element formulations—essential for convection-dominated or incompressible flows—remains challenging within the isogeometric analysis (IGA) framework. This gap limits the application of IGA's superior geometric fidelity to critical inverse problems in computational physics and engineering.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a variationally mimetic operator network (VMON) approach can be used to systematically derive and discretize the adjoint of a stabilized primal formulation, ensuring discrete consistency and yielding accurate, efficient design sensitivities for isogeometric optimization on complex trimmed and locally refined geometries.
proposals.method: We will first formulate the continuous adjoint equations for a model convection-diffusion problem with streamline-upwind/Petrov-Galerkin (SUPG) stabilization. Leveraging Hughes's recent work on variationally mimetic operator networks, we will construct discrete adjoint operators that preserve the mimetic structure of the stabilized primal system. The sensitivity analysis will be integrated with the trimmed NURBS and local refinement capabilities from his recent meshing papers, using algorithmic differentiation for the geometric parameterization. The full framework will be implemented and tested on inverse design benchmarks for nanofluidic channels (simulating nanoliposome transport) and magnetic domain wall (varmion) dynamics.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a mathematically consistent and computationally efficient adjoint framework for stabilized IGA, directly enabling high-fidelity inverse design in problems with dominant transport. It will extend the scope of isogeometric design optimization to a broader class of multiphysics problems. The integration of VMONs with adjoint calculus will also advance the theory of structure-preserving discretizations for optimization.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the adjoint method for stabilized formulations with the emerging concept of variationally mimetic operator networks, ensuring discrete adjoint consistency often lost in conventional stabilized methods. Furthermore, applying this to the inverse design of nanoscale systems (nanoliposomes, varmions) using IGA's geometric capabilities is an unexplored frontier.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks with Adaptive Isogeometric Analysis for Interface Problems
proposals.motivation: Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) and its extension to trimmed geometries via fixed, locally refined grids have advanced simulation accuracy, particularly for complex geometries. However, the assumption of a fixed background grid for integration limits efficiency and accuracy for evolving interface problems, such as phase-field models or fluid-structure interaction, where high resolution is dynamically required near interfaces. A method that retains the geometric exactness and superior approximation of IGA while enabling adaptive refinement is a critical unmet need in computational mechanics.
proposals.hypothesis: By hybridizing Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMINets) with adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) driven by a posteriori error estimation, we can create a framework that dynamically concentrates computational effort near interfaces while preserving the mimetic structure and stability guarantees of the underlying variational formulation.
proposals.method: We will develop an adaptive VarMINet framework where the neural operator is trained to approximate solution mappings on a coarse IGA spline space. A residual-based a posteriori error estimator, computed from the VarMINet output, will guide local h-refinement of the underlying NURBS mesh in regions of high error, particularly near interfaces. The refined geometry description will use the recent techniques for computing reaction forces on trimmed, locally refined meshes. The VarMINet will then be re-evaluated or fine-tuned on the adaptively enriched discretization, creating a closed-loop, accuracy-driven simulation pipeline.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, efficient computational framework that marries the data-driven efficiency of neural operators with the rigorous adaptivity of IGA, specifically targeting interface-dominated problems. It will extend the applicability of VarMINets to problems requiring dynamic spatial resolution, providing a blueprint for integrating machine learning with traditional adaptive finite element methods. The resulting method will demonstrate significantly improved accuracy per degree of freedom for benchmark problems in fracture mechanics or multiphase flow.
proposals.novelty: The integration of neural operators like VarMINets with rigorous, estimator-driven AMR for IGA is unexplored. The novelty lies in using the mimetic operator as a coarse, fast surrogate to guide adaptive refinement, inverting the traditional fixed-grid assumption of many deep learning-enhanced solvers, and leveraging recent advances in trimmed mesh analysis for practical implementation.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic Isogeometric Analysis Framework for High-Fidelity Eulerian Flow Simulations
proposals.motivation: Finite difference (FD) discretizations, while dominant in many computational fluid dynamics (CFD) codes, often struggle with complex geometries and enforcing conservation laws exactly on coarse meshes. The Eulerian formulation for transport problems requires high geometric fidelity and accurate flux computation, areas where traditional FD methods are limited. Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) and recent variationally mimetic frameworks offer a promising avenue for superior accuracy and structure preservation.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an Eulerian formulation implemented via a variationally mimetic operator network within an Isogeometric Analysis framework will yield more accurate and physically consistent solutions for reaction forces and fluxes than standard finite difference schemes, especially on trimmed and locally refined meshes.
proposals.method: The core Eulerian transport equations will be discretized using B-spline or NURBS basis functions within an IGA setting, leveraging Hughes' expertise. We will employ the concept of variationally mimetic operator networks (VMONs) to construct discrete differential operators that preserve key integral identities (e.g., the de Rham complex) by design. The method will be integrated with techniques for computing reaction forces on trimmed meshes and the use of quadrilateral layout generation for optimal parameterization of complex domains. Numerical integration will be performed on the locally refined meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, high-order accurate computational framework for Eulerian flow simulations that inherently respects conservation properties. It will provide a direct, rigorous comparison between state-of-the-art IGA/mimetic methods and conventional FD approaches, quantifying gains in accuracy per degree of freedom. The resulting software framework will be a testbed for high-fidelity simulation on complex geometries.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the variationally mimetic operator network paradigm—a structure-preserving discretization technique—with the geometric exactness and high-order smoothness of Isogeometric Analysis, specifically targeting the limitations of finite difference methods for Eulerian flow problems.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic Operator Network for 3D Eulerian Fluid-Structure Interaction in Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: Eulerian fluid-structure interaction (FSI) methods are crucial for simulating large, complex deformations and topological changes, but their extension to three dimensions remains computationally challenging and prone to numerical instability. Prior work, including the researcher's own on Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiON), has demonstrated promise for stable, accurate simulations in lower dimensions. A robust, high-fidelity 3D Eulerian FSI solver is needed for critical applications in biomedical engineering (e.g., heart valve dynamics) and aerospace, where pre-2026 methods often rely on costly Lagrangian mesh updates or simplified models.
proposals.hypothesis: The VarMiON architecture, which learns solution operators that preserve underlying variational structure, can be extended to provide a stable, efficient, and geometrically accurate framework for fully three-dimensional Eulerian FSI simulations when integrated with isogeometric analysis (IGA) on trimmed, locally refined meshes.
proposals.method: We will develop a 3D FSI solver by integrating the VarMiON framework with core Hughes-group methodologies. The fluid and structure domains will be represented using IGA splines on trimmed, adaptive Cartesian meshes generated via the equivalence class-based layout algorithm. The VarMiON will be trained to map from time-dependent boundary conditions and force inputs (the 'input' and 'varmion' components) to the coupled system's velocity and pressure fields, enforcing mimetic properties of the differential operators. The reaction force computation strategy for trimmed meshes will ensure accurate fluid-structure coupling. The network will be trained on data generated from high-fidelity IGA simulations of canonical 3D FSI benchmarks.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver the first publicly available, stable implementation of a fully 3D Eulerian FSI method using a variationally mimetic neural operator, significantly advancing simulation capability for problems with extreme deformation. It will provide a rigorous numerical analysis of the method's convergence and stability in 3D. Furthermore, it will demonstrate a novel synthesis of IGA, trimmed mesh technology, and operator learning, setting a new paradigm for physics-informed machine learning in computational mechanics.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the unique synthesis of the VarMiON (a structure-preserving neural operator) with the geometric exactness and analysis-suitability of IGA on complex 3D trimmed domains, creating a new computational paradigm that is neither a pure neural network nor a traditional Galerkin method, but a learned, mimetic hybrid.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for Inverse Identification of Hyperelastic Material Parameters from Fluid-Structure Interaction Data
proposals.motivation: Inverse identification of solid material parameters from flow-induced deformation data is critical for biomedical applications, such as characterizing arterial tissue or engineered biomaterials, but remains computationally challenging. Traditional methods often decouple the fluid and solid solvers, leading to inefficiency and loss of accuracy in the coupled sensitivity analysis required for optimization. A robust, high-fidelity inverse framework that respects the coupled physics is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework combining Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) for the forward fluid-structure interaction (FSI) model with a Variationally Mimetic Operator Network (VarMiON) for the inverse solver will enable accurate, stable, and computationally efficient identification of nonlinear hyperelastic material parameters from flow and deformation data.
proposals.method: The forward FSI model will be implemented using IGA on trimmed, locally refined meshes, providing high-order accuracy for both the fluid flow and solid deformation. The sensitivity of the solid deformation to material parameters will be computed via the adjoint method within this IGA framework. This adjoint-IGA solver will then be used to generate a high-fidelity dataset to train a VarMiON, a physics-informed neural operator architecture. The VarMiON will learn the mapping from observed deformation/flow fields to material parameters, acting as a fast, mesh-independent inverse surrogate.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, operator-based deep learning framework for inverse FSI problems, significantly accelerating parameter identification while maintaining the accuracy of high-order IGA. It will provide a concrete methodology for integrating adjoint-based sensitivity with mimetic neural operators, advancing the field of physics-informed machine learning for coupled multiphysics systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of three of Hughes' core themes: using IGA as the high-fidelity forward model, employing its adjoint for sensitivity, and deploying the recently proposed VarMiON architecture as a physics-consistent inverse surrogate, creating a new end-to-end pipeline for inverse material identification in FSI.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for Gradient-Based Shape Optimization in Fluid-Structure Interaction
proposals.motivation: Shape optimization for drag reduction in fluid-structure interaction (FSI) is computationally intensive, requiring repeated high-fidelity flow solutions and accurate sensitivity analysis. Traditional methods using isogeometric analysis (IGA) offer geometric exactness but can be costly within optimization loops, especially on complex, trimmed geometries. An efficient, accurate, and robust solver is needed to make high-resolution shape optimization tractable for practical engineering designs.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiONs), trained to solve the governing PDEs, can serve as a highly efficient and accurate surrogate solver within a gradient-based shape optimization framework, enabling rapid convergence to optimal shapes while preserving the geometric fidelity of isogeometric representations.
proposals.method: We will integrate VarMiONs, which learn solution operators for parameterized PDEs, into a shape optimization loop. The geometric design variables, defined using IGA splines, will serve as the network's input parameters. A VarMiON, pre-trained on high-fidelity IGA simulation data, will rapidly predict flow fields and output functionals (e.g., drag). We will compute shape sensitivities via automatic differentiation through the trained VarMiON, enabling efficient gradient-based optimization using algorithms like the method of moving asymptotes. The framework will be tested on benchmark FSI problems involving trimmed NURBS geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel optimization framework that combines the geometric precision of IGA with the computational speed of neural operator surrogates. It will demonstrate a significant reduction in computational cost for shape optimization cycles compared to conventional IGA-FSI solvers. The results will provide a pathway for applying machine learning-enhanced solvers to large-scale, high-fidelity design problems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight integration of a VarMiON—a structure-preserving neural operator—as the differentiable PDE solver within an IGA-based shape optimization loop, creating an end-to-end differentiable design pipeline. This approach moves beyond using neural networks as simple regression models, leveraging their operator-learning capability for sensitivity analysis.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Enrichment: Analytically-Tailored Bubbles for Singularities and Layers in Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: Standard finite element and isogeometric analysis (IGA) methods, while powerful, struggle with solution features like point singularities and sharp boundary layers, requiring excessive mesh refinement. Pre-2026, methods like extended B-splines for trimmed domains and residual-based error estimation exist, but a systematic, analytically-informed enrichment strategy within the IGA framework remains underdeveloped. This limits efficiency for problems in fracture mechanics, high-Peclet number flows, and composite material interfaces.
proposals.hypothesis: That constructing and incorporating problem-specific, non-polynomial bubble functions—derived from local asymptotic analysis and integrated via a variationally mimetic framework—will dramatically improve the coarse-mesh accuracy and convergence for PDEs with singular or layer-type solutions, without sacrificing numerical stability.
proposals.method: The approach integrates three components from Hughes' recent work: 1) A coarse-grid error indicator, inspired by flux computation on trimmed meshes, will flag 'problem' elements. 2) A local analytical solver will map asymptotic forms (e.g., log(r), r^β, exp(-y/ε)) to a parameterized library, generating tailored enrichment functions. 3) These bubbles will be incorporated using the Variationally Mimetic Operator Network (VarMiON) concept, ensuring a stable local variational formulation that preserves structure. The implementation will leverage IGA's geometric exactness and refinement capabilities.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a rigorous, automated framework for enriching IGA and FEM spaces with physics-aware functions, moving beyond heuristic enrichment. It will deliver a significant advance in coarse-mesh accuracy for challenging problems, directly impacting computational fracture and boundary layer simulation. The methodology bridges local analytical solution techniques with robust numerical implementation.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the closed-loop, automated process from error detection to analytical bubble construction and stable variational embedding, specifically within an isogeometric context. This goes beyond static enrichment or global a priori knowledge, creating a responsive, locally-tailored approximation space.

proposals.title: Isogeometric Shape Reconstruction via the Inverse Dirichlet-to-Neumann Map Using Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks
proposals.motivation: Inverse problems for unknown domain boundaries, such as detecting corrosion or tumor shapes from boundary measurements, are critical in non-destructive evaluation and medical imaging. The Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DtN) map encodes the domain's geometry, but its inversion is highly ill-posed and computationally challenging. Pre-2026 methods often rely on simplified parametrizations or linearizations that limit accuracy for complex, high-curvature boundaries.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a computational framework combining the high-order geometric fidelity of Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) with the stability and data-efficiency of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiONs) can robustly and accurately reconstruct unknown domain boundaries from partial DtN map data.
proposals.method: We will formulate the inverse problem as a PDE-constrained optimization, minimizing the discrepancy between the measured and computed DtN maps. The unknown boundary will be represented as a B-spline curve (for 2D) or surface (for 3D) within an IGA framework, enabling direct analysis-suitable geometry manipulation. The forward solver for the interior Laplace (or Helmholtz) problem, required at each optimization step, will be accelerated using a pre-trained VarMiON, which mimics the variational structure of the PDE for fast, accurate solutions on trimmed, locally refined meshes. The gradient of the objective with respect to the control point coordinates will be computed via the adjoint method.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, high-order accurate computational pipeline for geometric inverse problems, directly integrating shape representation and analysis. It will extend the application of VarMiONs to inverse problems and demonstrate a robust strategy for DtN inversion that leverages the strengths of IGA for shape optimization and deep operator networks for rapid forward solves.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the tight coupling of IGA's geometric language with the data-driven efficiency of VarMiONs for an inverse problem, moving beyond traditional finite element discretizations and linearized inversion schemes to enable the reconstruction of complex geometries from limited boundary data.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for Physics- and Mesh-Adaptive Stabilization in Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: In isogeometric analysis (IGA) and finite element methods, the selection of stabilization parameters (e.g., τ for SUPG) is critical for accuracy and stability, especially on complex, trimmed, or locally refined meshes. Analytical formulas for τ are often problem-specific, overly conservative, or fail to account for intricate mesh conditioning and solution features, limiting robustness for novel geometries and non-uniform discretizations. A general, adaptive framework is needed to ensure stability across the wide range of problems enabled by modern IGA and CAD integration.
proposals.hypothesis: A hybrid framework, combining a theoretically rigorous variational branch for conservative bounds with a data-driven neural operator branch, can learn to predict optimal, spatially varying stability parameter fields that generalize across complex geometries and mesh types, outperforming single analytical formulas.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-branch system. The theoretical branch will compute rigorous, element-wise upper bounds for local time scales using variationally mimetic operator theory, providing a conservative stability guarantee. Concurrently, a neural operator branch, inspired by architectures like DeepONet or Fourier Neural Operators (pre-2026), will be trained to map inputs—including geometric features from trimmed NURBS parameterizations, local mesh metrics, and coarse solution snapshots—to a correction field that refines the theoretical bound. The final τ field will be a safeguarded combination, ensuring stability while enhancing accuracy. Training data will be generated from high-fidelity simulations on diverse, trimmed IGA meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a robust, automated method for determining stabilization parameters in IGA, directly addressing challenges in trimmed mesh analysis and local refinement. It will advance operator learning by tightly coupling it with variational principles for guaranteed physics-based constraints. The outcome is a practical tool for enabling stable simulations on complex CAD geometries without manual parameter tuning.
proposals.novelty: The approach abandons the search for a universal analytical τ, instead learning an adaptive field, which is novel for stabilization in IGA. The tight integration of a variationally mimetic theoretical safeguard with a mesh-condition-aware neural operator creates a new paradigm for physics-informed, geometry-adaptive numerical stability.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic Framework for Intrinsic Time-Scale Parameters on Complex Trimmed and Locally Refined Geometries
proposals.motivation: The Generalized-α and other time integration methods rely on an intrinsic time-scale parameter τ, often derived analytically for simple elements and uniform meshes. For complex industrial geometries modeled with trimmed NURBS and local refinement, as common in Isogeometric Analysis (IGA), these analytical formulas break down, leading to suboptimal stability and accuracy in transient simulations. A robust, mesh-independent definition of τ is critical for advancing IGA for practical, time-dependent problems in fluid and solid mechanics.
proposals.hypothesis: By leveraging a variational mimetic philosophy and the concept of operator networks, a generalized, element-local τ can be derived directly from the discrete differential operators of the problem, ensuring stability and accuracy independent of mesh distortion, trimming, and local refinement.
proposals.method: We will extend the Variationally Mimetic Operator Network (VarMiON) framework to time-dependent problems. The method constructs local τ formulas using the spectral properties of the combined discrete differential operators (e.g., stiffness and mass matrices) on a per-element basis, informed by the local mesh geometry and spline basis. This will be integrated with the team's expertise in computing fluxes on trimmed meshes and quadrilateral layout optimization to handle complex parameterizations. The final algorithm will output a τ map over a non-uniform IGA mesh for use in stabilized time integrators.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, theoretically grounded procedure for computing stable time-step parameters for trimmed and locally refined IGA meshes. It will directly enable more reliable and efficient transient simulations of real-world engineering components. The output will be a practical software module integrable into existing IGA solvers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in synthesizing the variational mimetic approach with the practical challenges of trimmed IGA geometries, moving beyond classical τ derivations for canonical elements to a operator-driven definition resilient to complex mesh topologies.

proposals.title: A Data-Assimilation-Inspired Benchmark for Subgrid-Scale Models: Quantifying the Performance of Bubble Function and Stabilized Finite Element Methods
proposals.motivation: In computational fluid dynamics (CFD), selecting an appropriate subgrid-scale (SGS) model for under-resolved turbulent flow simulations remains a critical, yet often ad-hoc, decision. Bubble function enrichment and stabilized methods (like VMS) are two prominent finite element approaches, but a systematic, objective comparison of their accuracy and robustness against high-fidelity data is lacking. This gap hinders the optimal application of these methods in complex industrial and geophysical simulations where reliability is paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: By adapting the rigorous benchmarking framework of Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) to CFD, we hypothesize that the bubble function method will demonstrate superior spectral accuracy for specific turbulent scales, while stabilized methods will show greater robustness and computational efficiency across a broader range of benchmark flow regimes.
proposals.method: We will implement both SGS models within a common, open-source isogeometric analysis (IGA) solver framework, leveraging Hughes' expertise in splines and IGA. For a standardized set of benchmark flows (e.g., turbulent channel flow, flow past a cylinder), we will generate ensemble simulations with perturbed initial conditions. High-fidelity Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS) data will serve as the reference 'truth.' Performance will be quantified using comprehensive error metrics (L²/H¹ norms, spectral energy distributions) and skill scores (e.g., for mean drag and pressure) computed against the DNS, directly analogous to how NWP systems evaluate parameterizations against observations and high-resolution models.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce the first objective, data-driven benchmark for these fundamental SGS models, providing clear guidance for practitioners on their optimal application domains. It will establish a rigorous, reproducible verification methodology for SGS modeling that bridges concepts from data assimilation and numerical analysis, advancing the field beyond anecdotal comparison.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic translation of NWP's data-assimilation-based parameterization benchmarking to the core verification problem in CFD, and in the direct, high-fidelity-data-driven comparison of two philosophically distinct finite element SGS approaches within a modern IGA framework.

proposals.title: A Variational Multiscale Analysis: Systematic Comparison of Bubble-Enriched and Residual-Based Stabilized Approximations for Advection-Dominated Problems
proposals.motivation: Within the Variational Multiscale (VMS) framework, both bubble function enrichment and residual-based stabilization (e.g., SUPG, GLS) are established techniques for modeling subgrid scales and stabilizing advection-dominated flows. However, a rigorous, quantitative comparison of their approximation accuracy, particularly in the context of modern isogeometric analysis (IGA) and high-order spline bases, is lacking. This gap hinders the optimal selection and design of subgrid scale models for complex problems in computational fluid dynamics and transport.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given mesh and polynomial order, bubble function methods provide a more accurate representation of the fine-scale solution within an element compared to standard stabilized methods, but that this advantage diminishes in a quantifiable way for specific problem classes and can be linked to the structure of the variational multiscale splitting.
proposals.method: We will employ a unified VMS formulation to derive both bubble-based and residual-stabilized methods for a scalar advection-diffusion model problem. The analysis will be conducted using high-order B-spline and NURBS discretizations within an isogeometric framework, leveraging the group's expertise. We will implement both methods in a controlled codebase, systematically varying the Péclet number, element topology (including trimmed meshes), and approximation order. Accuracy will be quantified using fine-scale solution recovery techniques and rigorous a priori and a posteriori error estimates for the subgrid component.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first systematic, numerical evidence quantifying the accuracy trade-offs between these two major subgrid scale approximation families. It will yield practical guidelines for method selection in IGA and finite element simulations. Furthermore, it will establish a clearer theoretical link between bubble-enrichment and residual-based stabilization, informing the development of next-generation variationally mimetic operator networks.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct, controlled comparison within a modern isogeometric setting, moving beyond qualitative arguments to produce quantitative error spectra. This approach connects classical bubble function theory with contemporary stabilized methods and machine-learning-inspired mimetic operators.

proposals.title: A Mori-Zwanzig Framework for Memory-Embedded Subgrid-Scale Modeling in Isogeometric Analysis of Transient Flows
proposals.motivation: Current subgrid-scale (SGS) models for large-eddy simulation (LES) and multiscale PDEs often rely on eddy-viscosity or similarity assumptions that are local in time, neglecting the crucial memory effects of unresolved scales. This limits predictive accuracy for transitional flows, turbulence, and wave propagation in heterogeneous media. A rigorous, non-local closure model derived from first principles is needed to improve fidelity in computational mechanics.
proposals.hypothesis: By treating a high-fidelity, spatially discretized PDE system as a 'microscopic' dynamical system, the Mori-Zwanzig projection operator formalism can be adapted to formally derive a temporally non-local SGS model for the resolved coarse-scale variables, explicitly capturing memory effects.
proposals.method: We will start from a semi-discrete Galerkin formulation, typical in Isogeometric Analysis (IGA), for a model transient PDE (e.g., Navier-Stokes). A projection operator will be defined to separate resolved (coarse mesh) and unresolved (fine/subgrid) degrees of freedom. Applying the Mori-Zwanzig formalism yields a Generalized Langevin Equation for the resolved scales, containing a deterministic term, a memory integral, and a 'noise' term. We will employ techniques from 'Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks' to develop data-informed approximations for the resulting memory kernel, ensuring discrete conservation properties. The framework will be tested on turbulent channel flow and wave propagation in heterogeneous solids using trimmed, locally refined IGA meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a first-principles derivation of memory-dependent SGS models for continuum mechanics, moving beyond phenomenological closures. It will deliver a new theoretical framework for assessing closure approximations and a practical computational methodology for incorporating temporal non-locality into IGA-based solvers for multiscale problems.
proposals.novelty: While Mori-Zwanzig is established in statistical mechanics, its systematic application to derive spatially coarse-grained, memory-dependent closures for continuum PDEs discretized via IGA is novel. The integration with variationally mimetic learning for kernel approximation bridges rigorous formalism with computable models.

proposals.title: Joint Reconstruction of Evolving Domains and Subgrid Dynamics via Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks and Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: Many critical applications, such as corrosion propagation or cellular biomechanics, involve time-dependent PDEs on evolving domains with unresolved microscale physics. Pre-2026, methods for geometry inversion (e.g., via Dirichlet-to-Neumann maps) and data-driven subgrid modeling (e.g., via neural operators) have developed separately. A unified framework is needed to simultaneously identify the moving boundary and the missing fine-scale closure models from sparse macroscopic data, enabling full-system identification in complex multiscale environments.
proposals.hypothesis: That a synergistic framework combining variationally mimetic operator networks (VarMiONs) for data-driven subgrid modeling and isogeometric analysis (IGA) for geometry representation can accurately and stably solve the coupled inverse problem of reconstructing an evolving domain boundary and its transient subgrid-scale dynamics from boundary measurements alone.
proposals.method: We will leverage IGA, using B-spline/NURBS basis functions from Hughes' prior work, to parameterize the unknown domain evolution. The inverse problem will be formulated as a PDE-constrained optimization, minimizing the misfit in boundary data. The unknown subgrid model will be represented by a VarMiON, a structure-preserving neural operator architecture, ensuring stability for transient problems. The framework will be implemented using adjoint-based sensitivity analysis computed on trimmed, locally refined meshes to handle complex geometries efficiently, with optimization performed via gradient-based methods.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel computational framework for full-system identification in multiscale, moving-boundary problems. It will advance isogeometric analysis by integrating it with neural operators for inverse modeling, and extend the theory of VarMiONs to time-dependent, nonlinear subgrid closures. The resulting methodology will be demonstrated on benchmark problems in corrosion and bio-transport.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in the concurrent, tightly coupled inversion of both the macroscopic geometry and the microscopic constitutive model using a single variational framework, moving beyond standalone geometry tracking or offline model discovery. This is enabled by the unique compatibility of IGA's geometric exactness and VarMiON's structure-preserving discretization of operators.

proposals.title: A Practical Methodology Transfer: Constructing Localized Subgrid Closures via Approximate Dirichlet-to-Neumann Operator Techniques
proposals.motivation: In computational fluid dynamics, constructing accurate and stable subgrid-scale (SGS) models for large-eddy simulation (LES) remains a fundamental challenge, particularly for complex turbulent flows. Concurrently, the exterior boundary community has developed sophisticated techniques, such as rational approximations and learned operators, to construct coarse-grained Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DtN) maps that encapsulate far-field effects. This project aims to directly translate these mature, boundary-focused approximation tools to the interior closure problem, offering a new, systematic pathway for SGS model development.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that approximation techniques for constructing DtN maps on exterior domains can be systematically repurposed to formulate spatially compact, non-local-in-scale SGS closures, and that these derived models will demonstrate competitive or superior performance to classical eddy-viscosity and similarity models in well-defined benchmark flows.
proposals.method: We will establish a concrete pipeline: (1) Formalize the analogy between a coarse-scale LES cell and an exterior domain, defining a local 'boundary' for subgrid interactions. (2) Adapt specific DtN approximation families—including rational approximations (e.g., optimized Schwarz methods), boundary condition homogenization, and neural operators (inspired by Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks)—to construct explicit SGS stress models. (3) Implement these closures within an isogeometric analysis framework, leveraging trimmed NURBS and local refinement for accurate discretization. (4) Perform a direct, comparative assessment against Smagorinsky and dynamic models on canonical benchmarks (e.g., turbulent channel flow, periodic hill) to evaluate accuracy, stability, and computational cost.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a validated set of novel, hybrid SGS closure methodologies derived from a principled translation process, directly advancing LES capability. It will provide a clear, empirical understanding of which DtN approximation families are most effective for turbulence modeling, moving beyond abstract unification to yield practical simulation tools.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies not in a grand theoretical unification, but in the systematic, practical repurposing of mature boundary operator approximation tools—a distinct field—to generate concrete, new interior closure models, followed by a rigorous head-to-head performance evaluation against established LES approaches.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for Vision-Driven Dirichlet-to-Neumann Subgrid Modeling of Fluid-Structure Interaction
proposals.motivation: High-fidelity simulation of fluid-structure interaction (FSI), such as cardiac flow or flapping flight, is limited by the need to prescribe realistic, dynamic boundary motion. Pre-2026, subgrid modeling and DtN maps offer a rigorous framework for scale separation, but they typically rely on simplified or synthetic boundary kinematics. Integrating real-world, observed motion into these high-fidelity solvers remains a significant challenge, limiting predictive accuracy for complex biological and engineering systems.
proposals.hypothesis: That a dynamic Dirichlet-to-Neumann map, whose boundary condition is directly informed by high-resolution optical flow data extracted from imaging, can be variationally coupled to an interior turbulence model to create a boundary-aware, physics-consistent subgrid simulation paradigm with superior accuracy for FSI problems.
proposals.method: We will develop a framework where computer vision algorithms (e.g., Farnebäck or Horn-Schunck optical flow) process experimental video or 4D imaging data to extract time-dependent displacement fields on domain boundaries. This displacement data will be encoded as a spline-based boundary representation, compatible with isogeometric analysis (IGA). A DtN operator will be constructed on an exterior domain using variationally mimetic operator networks (VarMiONs) to ensure stability and exact conservation laws. This DtN map will provide a dynamic boundary condition for an interior IGA-based Navier-Stokes solver employing a variational multiscale (VMS) turbulence model, ensuring energy-consistent coupling.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational methodology that fuses data-driven boundary kinematics with rigorous mathematical modeling for FSI. It will advance the field of isogeometric analysis by providing a systematic pipeline from imaging to simulation. The resulting open-source framework will enable highly accurate simulations for biomedical and aerospace applications where boundary motion is complex and experimentally derived.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious fusion lies in using computer vision not for model correction or assimilation post-simulation, but to directly define the fundamental boundary operator (the DtN map) of a high-fidelity subgrid model, creating a new paradigm where the simulation's exterior boundary is a 'digital twin' of observed physics.

proposals.title: A Renormalization Group Framework for the Systematic Analysis and Comparison of Subgrid Scale Models in Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: Subgrid scale (SGS) approximations, such as bubble function and stabilized methods, are essential for simulating multiscale phenomena in fluid and solid mechanics. However, selecting and optimizing these models remains largely heuristic, relying on numerical experiment rather than a fundamental theory of their scale-separation behavior. A rigorous, physics-based framework is needed to quantify how different SGS models distort the transfer of energy across scales, which is critical for accuracy in turbulent flows and composite material analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The renormalization group (RG) coarse-graining procedure provides a rigorous, scale-by-scale decomposition against which the error introduced by any SGS approximation can be quantified as a function of the resolved wavelength, enabling a fundamental comparison of their effective dynamics and energy transfer properties.
proposals.method: We will develop an RG framework for isogeometric analysis (IGA) by constructing a wavelet or hierarchical B-spline basis to explicitly separate solution scales. The coarse-graining procedure will be implemented via variational projection, defining exact and model-dependent RG flows for scale-to-scale couplings. We will integrate concepts from Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks to ensure discrete conservation laws are preserved in the coarse-grained dynamics. The error of specific models (e.g., VMS, GLS, bubble functions) will be quantified by comparing their RG flow parameters (effective viscosities, forces) to the exact flow derived from fine-scale IGA solutions.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first principled, theoretical tool for ranking, optimizing, and hybridizing SGS models based on their RG flow behavior, moving beyond ad-hoc calibration. It will yield new error estimators that are sensitive to scale-transfer distortion and offer a pathway to constructing SGS models that preserve the statistical physics of the unresolved scales within an IGA setting.
proposals.novelty: While RG theory is foundational in statistical physics, its application as a direct numerical analysis tool for comparing finite element SGS models is novel. The fusion of RG coarse-graining with the smooth, high-order approximation spaces of IGA and mimetic operator networks creates a unique framework for accuracy analysis.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for High-Frequency Wave Propagation in the Resolved and Under-Resolved Regimes
proposals.motivation: High-frequency wave propagation, where the wavelength approaches the computational mesh size, presents a significant challenge for classical finite element methods, leading to numerical dispersion and pollution errors. Isogeometric analysis (IGA) offers superior accuracy per degree of freedom but remains computationally expensive for direct simulation at high frequencies. A framework is needed to bridge the gap between geometric flexibility and efficient, accurate wave simulation in multiscale settings.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating the geometric exactness of isogeometric analysis with the structure-preserving learning framework of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiON), we can develop a surrogate model that accurately captures wave physics in both resolved and under-resolved regimes, significantly reducing the cost of high-frequency simulations on complex geometries.
proposals.method: We will extend the VarMiON architecture, which maps input functions (e.g., material parameters, source terms) to solution fields via a variational mimetic layer, to the high-frequency Helmholtz and time-harmonic Maxwell's equations. The method will leverage IGA's smooth spline basis functions defined on trimmed, locally refined meshes (from recent work on reaction forces and quadrilateral layouts) to generate high-fidelity training data. The network's decoder will use B-spline basis functions, ensuring compatibility with the isogeometric framework and enabling efficient handling of complex geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel, geometry-aware neural operator for high-frequency wave problems, demonstrating superior accuracy and efficiency compared to standard IGA at comparable resolutions. It will provide a foundational methodology for applying physics-informed operator learning to wave propagation in the challenging regime where traditional numerical methods struggle.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of VarMiON's structure-preserving learning with the geometric precision of isogeometric analysis, specifically targeting the high-frequency regime. This moves beyond applying neural operators to low-frequency PDEs and directly addresses the core numerical challenges of wave pollution and dispersion.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic Operator Network for Coupling Active Fiber-Reinforced Ventricular Mechanics to Blood Flow
proposals.motivation: Accurately simulating the coupled electromechanics and hemodynamics of the heart is a grand challenge in computational physiology, critical for understanding disease and designing interventions. Existing fluid-structure interaction (FSI) models often simplify the myocardium as a passive, isotropic material, neglecting its intrinsically active, anisotropic, and fiber-reinforced composite nature, which is essential for capturing physiological pressure-volume loops and flow patterns.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by modeling the ventricular wall as an active, fiber-reinforced composite material—analogous to aerospace composite models—and coupling it to a blood flow solver using a variationally mimetic framework, we can achieve a more physiologically accurate and numerically robust simulation of whole-chamber cardiac function.
proposals.method: We will develop an isogeometric analysis (IGA) framework using trimmed NURBS for the ventricular geometry. The constitutive model will combine a passive anisotropic Holzapfel-Ogden-type hyperelastic law with an active tension model along local fiber directions from a phenomenological electrophysiology input. The coupling to an IGA-based incompressible Navier-Stokes solver for blood flow will be managed via a variationally mimetic operator network (VMON), ensuring stability by preserving underlying mathematical structures. The system will be solved monolithically using a Newton-Raphson scheme on locally refined meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, high-fidelity computational framework for cardiac electromechanics-FSI that faithfully represents the myocardium's composite architecture. It will provide new insights into the interplay between active fiber contraction, wall deformation, and intraventricular flow, serving as a powerful tool for in-silico cardiology. The application of VMONs to this strongly coupled multiphysics problem advances numerical methodology for complex biomedical FSI.
proposals.novelty: The key novelty is the explicit analogy drawn from aerospace composite modeling to formulate an active, anisotropic composite model for the ventricle, coupled with the application of the emerging VMON concept to ensure a stable, variationally consistent coupling between the active solid and fluid solvers within an IGA setting.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic, Strongly-Coupled Immersed Isogeometric Framework for High-Stiffness Fluid-Structure Interaction
proposals.motivation: Immersed Boundary Methods (IBMs) are powerful for complex geometries but are typically limited by explicit coupling and stability constraints (the CFL condition) when simulating stiff or rapidly moving structures, such as bio-inspired flapping wings or prosthetic heart valves. This forces prohibitively small time steps, negating IBM's efficiency advantages. A robust, implicit, strongly-coupled IBM that maintains the geometric flexibility of immersion while allowing for fluid-solver-sized time steps is a critical unsolved challenge in computational mechanics.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding a partitioned, strongly-coupled implicit fluid-structure interaction solver within an immersed framework and enforcing interface conditions via a variationally mimetic projection, we can achieve unconditional stability for stiff materials while exactly conserving momentum and satisfying the no-slip condition.
proposals.method: We will develop an implicit IBM where the fluid (Navier-Stokes) and structure (nonlinear elasticity) are solved on separate, non-matching meshes. The coupling will be enforced via a fixed-point iteration within each time step, using a Lagrange multiplier field defined on the immersed boundary to represent the fluid-structure traction. Inspired by Hughes's recent work on Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks, we will design a projection operator to transfer quantities between the fluid mesh and the structural spline surface (modeled using Isogeometric Analysis) that preserves conservation properties. The overall system will be solved using a Newton-Krylov method.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new computational framework that combines the geometric flexibility of immersed methods with the stability of implicit, strong coupling. It will enable the efficient simulation of previously intractable problems involving very stiff or fast-moving immersed bodies. The method will be demonstrated on benchmark problems and applied to simulate a dynamically pitching airfoil and a simplified mitral valve.
proposals.novelty: The integration of a strongly-coupled, implicit partitioned FSI solver within an IBM framework is a significant departure from standard explicit IBMs. The use of a variationally mimetic projection, rooted in structure-preserving discretization principles, to handle the interface coupling in this context is a novel and non-obvious synthesis of Hughes's recent methodological themes.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic, Non-Local Framework for Immersed Boundary-Large Eddy Simulation of Bio-Inspired Turbulent Flows
proposals.motivation: Predictive simulation of turbulent flows with complex moving boundaries, such as flapping wings or swimming fish, remains a grand challenge. Traditional immersed boundary (IB) methods use local force spreading, while standard Large Eddy Simulation (LES) subgrid-scale (SGS) models are purely local closures. This fails to capture the essential non-local, bi-directional scale interactions between a moving body's wake and the surrounding turbulence, limiting accuracy for fundamental and engineering applications.
proposals.hypothesis: Formulating both the IB force distribution and the SGS stress closure as non-local integral operators, derived from a unified variational principle, will create a physically consistent framework that significantly improves the prediction of forces and wake dynamics in turbulent flows with intricate motion.
proposals.method: We will develop the framework within an isogeometric analysis (IGA) setting, leveraging its high-order accuracy and geometric flexibility. The core innovation is the construction of coupled, non-local kernels: one for the IB force distribution and one for the SGS stress, inspired by the structure of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VMONs). These kernels will be defined over spatially adaptive domains informed by local flow features and boundary geometry, implemented efficiently using quadrature on trimmed, locally refined NURBS meshes. The entire system will be derived from a single, constrained variational principle to ensure stability and conservation properties.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, mathematically coherent computational methodology for turbulent fluid-structure interaction. It will provide the first IB-LES framework where the boundary dynamics and turbulence model are coupled through non-local physics, enabling high-fidelity simulations of bio-fluidic systems. The integration of IGA, VMONs, and non-local closures represents a significant advance in predictive computational mechanics.
proposals.novelty: The synthesis of non-local integral operators for both immersed boundary forcing and subgrid-scale stress within a single variational framework is unprecedented. Moving beyond the ad-hoc combination of local IB and local LES models, this approach directly encodes the physical two-way coupling of scales induced by moving boundaries.

proposals.title: A Probabilistic Intrinsic Time Scale Framework for Uncertainty Quantification in Isogeometric and Variationally Mimetic Discretizations
proposals.motivation: The intrinsic time scale parameter τ is fundamental for stability in stabilized finite element methods, but its deterministic formulation becomes unreliable for complex discretizations like trimmed NURBS in isogeometric analysis (IGA) or operator networks on non-uniform meshes. In these settings, geometric uncertainty, mesh sensitivity, and parameter variability are significant, yet current methods lack a principled way to quantify their impact on τ and, consequently, on solution accuracy and solver robustness for problems in fluid-structure interaction and material modeling.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by reformulating τ as a probabilistic quantity derived from a stochastic variational framework, we can rigorously quantify its uncertainty and propagate this to solution outputs, enabling adaptive solvers to make time-step and refinement choices with statistically guaranteed confidence levels.
proposals.method: We will extend the Variationally Mimetic Operator Network (VarMiON) concept to learn a mapping from distributions of geometric and discretization parameters (e.g., from trimmed IGA layouts or quadrilateral mesh optimization) to a distribution for τ. This builds on the concept of 'varmion' architectures for differential operators. Using polynomial chaos expansions or Bayesian inference, we will construct a stochastic τ model. Its propagation will be analyzed within a generalized-α time integration scheme, with adaptive decisions governed by the quantified uncertainty, directly leveraging Hughes' work on accurate force computation on trimmed meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel UQ-aware time integration theory for complex discretizations, providing a practical tool for robust adaptive solving. It will directly enhance the predictive reliability of IGA and mimetic methods in engineering applications with uncertain geometries and inputs, bridging the gap between advanced discretization research and rigorous uncertainty quantification.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in applying UQ not to the solution field directly, but to the fundamental stability parameter τ itself, and implementing this within the context of modern, geometry-sensitive discretization tools like trimmed IGA and variationally mimetic networks, which are active pre-2026 research themes of the investigator.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic AMR for Sub-Cellular Force Mapping in Biomechanical Flows
proposals.motivation: Understanding sub-cellular force distributions, such as shear stress gradients on cell membranes or around organelles, is critical for modeling mechanotransduction, drug delivery via nanoliposomes, and cellular damage. However, direct numerical simulation at these scales within a tissue context is computationally prohibitive. An efficient, high-fidelity method is needed to bridge organ-scale flow simulations with sub-cellular biomechanical detail.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that an adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) strategy, guided by variationally mimetic error estimators and built upon an isogeometric analysis (IGA) framework, can efficiently and accurately resolve sub-cellular force fields by dynamically concentrating computational effort only in regions of high biomechanical gradient.
proposals.method: We will develop an AMR algorithm within an IGA setting, using trimmed NURBS for geometric fidelity to complex cellular boundaries. Refinement will be driven by a posteriori error estimation based on the principles of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks, targeting local shear stress gradients and flow curvature. The core innovation is the integration of this error estimator with the 'quadrilateral layout generation using equivalence classes' method to manage the parametric domain of trimmed surfaces, ensuring robust and topology-aware mesh adaptation specifically for biological geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a novel computational framework for hybrid-scale biomechanical simulation, enabling the first high-resolution maps of sub-cellular forces within organ-scale models. It will provide a rigorous, variationally consistent method for adaptive refinement in biological IGA, advancing computational biomechanics and the design of targeted therapeutic carriers.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of Hughes-group techniques—variationally mimetic operators, trimmed IGA, and quadrilateral layout optimization—to create a new AMR paradigm specifically for discontinuous biological geometries, moving beyond classical AMR used in aerospace or structured biological meshes.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Inversion of Hemodynamic Waveforms for Non-Invasive Quantification of Distributed Arterial Stiffness
proposals.motivation: Local arterial wall stiffness is a critical biomarker for cardiovascular diseases like atherosclerosis and aneurysms, but current non-invasive clinical methods (e.g., pulse wave velocity) provide only global or segment-averaged estimates. A method to quantify the distributed, passive stiffness along a specific arterial segment from standard clinical waveform data would enable earlier detection and localized monitoring of vascular pathology. This represents an unsolved inverse problem in cardiovascular biomechanics.
