proposals.hypothesis: The distributed elastic properties of an arterial wall segment can be uniquely identified by inverting a high-fidelity, isogeometric analysis (IGA) forward model of pulse wave propagation against clinically observed blood pressure and flow waveforms, using a variational mimetic framework to ensure stability.
proposals.method: We will develop a computational pipeline using isogeometric analysis to construct the forward 1D/3D fluid-structure interaction model of hemodynamics in a patient-specific arterial segment. The inverse problem will be solved using a gradient-based optimization algorithm, where the gradient of the misfit function (difference between simulated and clinical waveforms) with respect to wall constitutive parameters is computed efficiently via the adjoint method. Critically, the parameterization of stiffness and the discretization of the forward and adjoint problems will be built using Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VMONs) to preserve key mathematical structures (e.g., conservation, symmetries) for robust and physically consistent inversion, even on trimmed, locally refined NURBS meshes derived from medical imaging.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, non-invasive computational diagnostic tool for quantifying localized arterial stiffness. It will advance the field of cardiovascular biomechanics by providing a rigorous inverse problem framework adapted from geophysics. The methodology will also demonstrate the utility of variationally mimetic discretizations in stabilizing complex biophysical inverse problems.
proposals.novelty: The adaptation of full-waveform inversion techniques from seismic prospecting to hemodynamic waveform analysis is novel. Furthermore, the integration of VMONs within an IGA-based inverse problem setting to ensure stable gradient computation for biomechanical property identification is a significant methodological innovation beyond traditional finite element approaches.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for the Inverse Estimation of Passive Arterial Wall Stiffness from Fluid-Structure Interaction Data
proposals.motivation: Estimating regional arterial wall stiffness is critical for diagnosing and monitoring cardiovascular diseases, but direct in vivo measurement is impossible. Current inverse methods, which infer material properties from observed motion and flow, often rely on simplified models or are computationally prohibitive for patient-specific, three-dimensional geometries. A robust, efficient, and geometrically precise inverse solver is needed to translate clinical imaging into quantitative biomarkers of vascular health.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework combining the geometric exactness of isogeometric analysis (IGA) with the data-efficient learning of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiONs) can accurately and stably solve the ill-posed inverse problem of estimating heterogeneous, passive arterial wall stiffness from sparse displacement and flow data.
proposals.method: We will develop an IGA-based forward solver for fluid-structure interaction (FSI) using trimmed, locally refined NURBS meshes to accurately compute reaction forces on complex arterial geometries. The inverse problem will be posed as a PDE-constrained optimization, minimizing the discrepancy between simulated and observed vessel wall motion. To overcome the high computational cost of repeated forward solves during optimization, we will employ a VarMiON as a surrogate model. This network, trained on a dataset of FSI solutions parameterized by spatial stiffness fields, will learn the solution operator in a manner that preserves key variational structures, ensuring stability for the inverse query.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel computational pipeline for the non-invasive, image-based estimation of patient-specific arterial stiffness. It will advance inverse problem methodology by demonstrating the integration of structure-preserving neural operators with high-fidelity IGA forward models. The resulting tool will provide a foundation for more reliable computational hemodynamics assessments in clinical research.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the unique synthesis of Hughes's core expertise: using isogeometric analysis for geometric fidelity and solution accuracy, while applying the emerging VarMiON architecture—a structure-preserving neural operator—to create a stable and efficient inverse solver for a complex multiphysics problem, moving beyond traditional adjoint-based or purely data-driven approaches.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for PDE-Constrained Inverse Problems in Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: Inverse problems, such as shape optimization or parameter identification in PDEs, are computationally intensive and require high-fidelity geometric representations. Pre-2026 isogeometric analysis (IGA) provides a unified framework for design and analysis, but its application to inverse problems is hindered by the need for efficient, accurate, and stable sensitivity analysis, especially on complex trimmed and locally refined meshes common in CAD. A robust method is needed to bridge this gap.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that integrating the mathematical structure of variationally mimetic operator networks (VMONs) with isogeometric analysis will yield a novel, stable, and geometrically exact framework for solving PDE-constrained inverse problems, significantly improving gradient computation and convergence.
proposals.method: The method will extend the VMON architecture to enforce discrete conservation laws and differential operator symmetries within an IGA setting. We will develop an adjoint-based sensitivity analysis using the concept of reaction forces on trimmed NURBS patches, building on recent work for computing fluxes. The optimization loop will employ gradient-based algorithms, where the VMON acts as a differentiable surrogate for the state and adjoint equations, ensuring consistency with the underlying variational structure of the PDE constraint.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, principled computational framework for inverse problems that preserves geometric exactness from CAD and the variational structure of the physics. It will provide a concrete algorithm for stable shape and parameter optimization on complex trimmed domains, advancing the integration of IGA into automated design and identification workflows.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the mimetic operator network philosophy—which embeds mathematical structure into neural architectures—with the geometric precision of IGA for inverse problems, moving beyond standard neural PDE surrogates that lack guaranteed conservation properties and geometric fidelity.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Damping Models for Isogeometric Vibration Analysis of Rotation-Free Beams and Plates
proposals.motivation: Isogeometric analysis (IGA) has proven highly effective for the dynamic analysis of thin structures using rotation-free formulations, offering superior accuracy and smoothness. However, realistic vibration modeling for applications like MEMS, flexible aerospace components, and biomechanical structures requires accurate damping models to predict energy dissipation and transient response. Extending the recent rotationless IGA beam and plate models to include damping remains an open challenge, critical for predictive simulation.
proposals.hypothesis: That viscous and structural damping mechanisms can be consistently incorporated into rotation-free isogeometric vibration models using a variationally mimetic framework, leading to stable, accurate, and physically meaningful predictions of damped dynamic response without spurious numerical dissipation.
proposals.method: We will extend the variational foundation of the rotation-free models by incorporating Rayleigh-type damping functionals, introducing velocity-dependent terms into the weak form. The damping matrix will be constructed using a variationally mimetic approach, ensuring it is derived from the same function spaces and quadrature rules as the mass and stiffness matrices to preserve spectral properties. For complex geometries, we will leverage trimmed NURBS and local refinement strategies to compute damping forces accurately on trimmed meshes. The implementation will be validated against analytical solutions for simple beams and plates.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first rigorously formulated damping model for rotation-free IGA structural dynamics, enabling high-fidelity transient and frequency-domain analysis of damped systems. It will deliver a practical computational framework, integrated with existing isogeometric workflows, for simulating the realistic dynamic behavior of slender structures in engineering design.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the variationally mimetic operator network philosophy with damping modeling, ensuring a consistent variational structure for the complete damped system, and the application of this framework to the specific context of rotation-free IGA formulations, which lack rotational degrees of freedom for direct damping assignment.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Isogeometric Analysis for High-Fidelity Fluid-Structure Interaction Vibration Analysis
proposals.motivation: The accurate simulation of fluid-structure interaction (FSI) vibrations is critical for the design of aerospace components, biomedical devices, and offshore structures. Pre-2026 isogeometric analysis (IGA) has excelled in structural and fluid mechanics separately, but robust, high-order monolithic coupling for vibration problems remains a challenge, especially on complex trimmed and locally refined geometries common in engineering. A unified, variationally consistent framework is needed to capture coupled eigenfrequencies and damping effects without spurious energy losses at the interface.
proposals.hypothesis: By extending the Variationally Mimetic Operator Network (VarMiON) architecture to a monolithic FSI setting, we can construct a geometrically exact, high-order discretization that preserves the mimetic properties of the coupled system, leading to superior spectral accuracy for vibration analysis compared to traditional partitioned or low-order monolithic methods.
proposals.method: We will develop a monolithic isogeometric formulation for the linearized FSI eigenvalue problem. The structural domain will use IGA solid shells or Kirchhoff-Love shells, while the fluid will be modeled using a potential flow or linearized Navier-Stokes IGA formulation. The core innovation is the application of the VarMiON framework to learn and enforce the variationally mimetic coupling conditions at the fluid-structure interface, ensuring exact conservation properties. This approach will be integrated with the concepts of reaction force computation on trimmed meshes and quadrilateral layout optimization for the interface to handle complex geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, robust computational framework for high-fidelity FSI vibration analysis, directly applicable to design optimization. It will advance IGA by providing a principled method for monolithic coupling that preserves mimetic properties, and it will extend the VarMiON concept from single-physics to multi-physics systems.
proposals.novelty: The novel integration of the VarMiON architecture for enforcing interface conditions in a monolithic IGA-FSI setting, combined with the use of advanced geometry handling techniques (trimming, layout optimization) specifically for vibration problems, moves beyond existing loosely coupled or low-order monolithic IGA-FSI approaches.

proposals.title: A Systematic Comparison of k-Refinement in Isogeometric Analysis and High-Order p-FEM for Higher Mode Accuracy
proposals.motivation: Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) introduced k-refinement, which combines order elevation and knot insertion in a unique sequence, offering potential advantages in spectral accuracy and per-degree-of-freedom efficiency over traditional high-order Finite Element Methods (p-FEM). However, pre-2026 literature lacks a rigorous, systematic study isolating the performance of k-refinement specifically for capturing higher eigenmodes and high-frequency solution components, which are critical for wave propagation, vibration, and stability analyses. A definitive comparison is needed to quantify the practical benefits and guide method selection.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for a given polynomial degree and number of degrees of freedom, the k-refinement process in IGA will yield superior accuracy for higher eigenmodes and high-frequency solution features compared to standard p-FEM, due to the enhanced inter-element continuity and optimized basis function support inherent in the B-spline/NURBS construction.
proposals.method: We will conduct controlled numerical experiments on canonical 2D and 3D benchmark problems for linear elasticity and acoustic Helmholtz equations. We will implement a framework using pre-2026 IGA libraries (e.g., PetIGA, GeoPDEs) and high-order FEM codes (e.g., MFEM, Nektar++) to perform matched-degree and matched-DOF comparisons. The study will employ the concept of variationally mimetic operator networks to ensure a consistent variational framework for error analysis. Accuracy will be measured using normalized errors in eigenvalue spectra and L²/H¹ norms for forced high-frequency problems, with detailed convergence studies and computational cost assessments.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the community with a clear, quantitative assessment of k-refinement's advantages and limitations for high-mode accuracy, filling a significant gap in the IGA literature. It will yield practical guidelines for engineers and scientists choosing between IGA and high-order FEM for wave-dominated and dynamic problems. The findings will also inform future development of spline-based discretizations for computational wave mechanics.
proposals.novelty: While general comparisons of IGA and FEM exist, a focused investigation isolating the *k-refinement* strategy—distinct from simple p-refinement with increased continuity—against p-FEM for higher-mode performance is novel. The systematic use of variationally mimetic principles to ensure a fair variational comparison framework adds further methodological rigor.

proposals.title: A Comparative Spectral Accuracy Study: Isogeometric k-Refinement versus High-Order Spectral Element Methods
proposals.motivation: Isogeometric analysis (IGA), pioneered by Hughes and colleagues, introduced k-refinement, which elevates both continuity and polynomial degree, promising superior spectral accuracy per degree-of-freedom compared to classical finite elements. However, a rigorous, high-fidelity benchmark against the gold standard for high-order methods—spectral element methods (SEMs)—is lacking. Establishing this benchmark is crucial for quantifying the true advantage of IGA's smooth basis functions in wave propagation, vibration, and fluid stability problems where accurate eigenvalue and dispersion properties are paramount.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for smooth problems, isogeometric k-refinement will achieve spectral convergence rates comparable to or exceeding those of SEMs for a given polynomial degree, but with superior accuracy per degree-of-freedom due to its higher inter-element continuity, particularly for eigenvalue and Helmholtz problems.
proposals.method: We will implement a controlled comparison framework using the open-source library NURBS-Python for IGA and the SEM library Nektar++ (pre-2026 versions). The study will solve canonical 2D and 3D elliptic (Poisson), Helmholtz, and eigenvalue (vibration) problems on simple geometries. For IGA, we will employ k-refinement on single-patch NURBS meshes. For SEM, we will use high-order Lagrange polynomials with Gauss-Lobatto-Legendre quadrature on geometrically conforming meshes. Accuracy will be measured via L2 and H1 error norms and eigenvalue error against analytic solutions, with convergence studied as a function of polynomial degree (p) and total degrees of freedom.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide the first direct, high-order benchmark quantifying the spectral accuracy of IGA's k-refinement against a mature SEM implementation. It will yield clear guidelines on the regimes (problem type, polynomial degree) where IGA's smoothness provides a decisive advantage. The results will serve as a foundational reference for the high-order finite element community and inform method selection for wave-based and vibration simulations.
proposals.novelty: While both IGA and SEM are well-studied independently, a systematic, like-for-like comparison focusing on spectral accuracy—informed by Hughes's recent work on variationally mimetic operators and accurate flux computation—is novel. The analysis will specifically isolate the effect of inter-element continuity (C^0 vs. C^{p-1}) from other factors, a non-obvious distinction often conflated in simpler p-refinement studies.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Reduced-Order Modeling for Fast Vibration Analysis in Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) provides high-fidelity simulation of structural dynamics but incurs high computational cost for repeated analyses, such as in design optimization or uncertainty quantification. While model order reduction (MOR) via Proper Orthogonal Decomposition (POD) is a standard acceleration technique, its direct application to IGA can compromise the geometric exactness and variational structure that are central to the method's accuracy and stability.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that by integrating the variational mimetic operator network (VarMiON) framework with IGA-based POD, we can construct structure-preserving reduced-order models (ROMs) that retain geometric fidelity and yield stable, long-time accurate predictions for linear and nonlinear vibration problems.
proposals.method: The method will first generate high-fidelity IGA snapshots for parameterized vibration problems using trimmed, locally refined meshes. A POD basis will be extracted from these snapshots. Crucially, the projection into the reduced space will be performed using the VarMiON architecture, which learns discrete mimetic operators that preserve key differential and algebraic structures of the full IGA system. This ensures the ROM inherits stability properties. The approach will be tested on problems involving complex geometries defined by multiple NURBS patches.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a new, efficient ROM framework for IGA dynamics that is both geometrically exact and variationally consistent. It will enable rapid, reliable vibration analysis for complex CAD-based designs, directly impacting fields like aerospace and automotive engineering where design cycles require numerous simulations. The integration of VarMiON provides a principled, physics-informed machine learning approach to MOR.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the VarMiON architecture with IGA-based MOR, creating structure-preserving ROMs that maintain the core advantages of IGA—exact geometry and high-order continuity—while achieving orders-of-magnitude speedup, a combination not yet fully explored in pre-2026 literature.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Isogeometric Analysis for Piezoelectric Scattering: A Coupled FEM-BEM Formulation for Unbounded Domains
proposals.motivation: Modeling piezoelectric devices like sensors and energy harvesters often requires simulating wave propagation or static fields in infinite media, a challenge for standard Finite Element Methods (FEM). Pre-2026, coupling FEM with the Boundary Element Method (BEM) is a recognized strategy for unbounded domains, but its integration with the geometric exactness and high-order smoothness of Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) for coupled piezoelectric problems remains underdeveloped. This limits the accurate and efficient simulation of wave-structure interaction in advanced transducer design.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a variationally mimetic operator network framework, integrating IGA for the near-field piezoelectric structure with a BEM for the infinite elastic/acoustic domain, will yield a stable, high-order accurate, and geometrically precise computational scheme for unbounded piezoelectric problems.
proposals.method: The method will leverage Hughes's recent work on Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VMONs) to derive a consistent coupling formulation. We will use IGA based on NURBS or T-splines for the piezoelectric domain, ensuring high-order continuity. For the unbounded domain, a collocation or Galerkin BEM with NURBS-based geometry description will be employed, using the fundamental solution for piezoelectrics. The coupling will be enforced weakly at the interface using a variationally mimetic approach, ensuring stability. The implementation will utilize concepts from trimmed meshes and layout generation for handling complex interface geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will provide a novel, high-fidelity computational tool for simulating piezoelectric devices in infinite media, directly impacting transducer and sensor design. It will extend the IGA paradigm to a new class of coupled domain problems and demonstrate the utility of variationally mimetic frameworks for integrating disparate numerical methods.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the seamless integration of IGA's geometric and analytical strengths with BEM for piezoelectricity within a variationally mimetic framework, moving beyond traditional FEM-BEM coupling to achieve higher-order accuracy and exact geometry representation for this multiphysics problem.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Adaptive LES: An Isogeometric Framework for Efficient Turbulent Flow Simulation on Trimmed, Locally Refined Meshes
proposals.motivation: The Variational Multiscale approach to Large Eddy Simulation (VMS-LES) is a powerful framework for turbulent flows, but its computational cost remains high for complex geometries and high Reynolds numbers. Adaptive mesh refinement (AMR) is essential for efficiency, yet its robust integration with VMS-LES and isogeometric analysis (IGA) on trimmed CAD models—common in engineering—is an unresolved challenge, limiting predictive design capabilities.
proposals.hypothesis: By integrating a variationally mimetic operator network (VMON) architecture as an a posteriori error estimator to drive adaptive refinement, a VMS-LES formulation can be made significantly more computationally efficient on trimmed, locally refined isogeometric meshes without sacrificing solution accuracy or stability.
proposals.method: We will develop an isogeometric VMS-LES formulation for the Navier-Stokes equations on trimmed domains, leveraging recent work on computing reaction forces on such meshes. The core innovation is using a trained VMON, which mimics key variational operators, to process local solution fields and predict sub-grid scale energy or error contributions. This VMON output will drive an h-refinement strategy based on equivalence classes of integral curves to generate optimal quadrilateral layout updates in regions of high error or turbulence activity, creating a closed-loop adaptive solve-estimate-refine cycle.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new, computationally efficient adaptive framework for high-fidelity turbulent flow simulation directly on CAD geometries. It will advance isogeometric analysis by providing a robust AMR methodology for industrial-scale LES and demonstrate a novel, physics-informed application of neural operator networks for numerical analysis.
proposals.novelty: The non-obvious synthesis lies in using a variationally mimetic network—a structure preserving neural operator—as the error estimator within an AMR loop for a nonlinear multiscale problem, and executing this entire pipeline on the complex trimmed meshes inherent to practical isogeometric analysis.

proposals.title: A Scalable Variationally Mimetic Operator Network for Industrial-Scale Turbulent Flow Simulations
proposals.motivation: Industrial computational fluid dynamics (CFD) for complex geometries, such as turbomachinery or full aircraft, requires high-fidelity simulations on massively parallel architectures. Traditional Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) and finite element methods face significant challenges in scaling efficiently on trimmed, locally refined meshes common in CAD, particularly in maintaining stability and accurate flux calculations. Bridging the gap between advanced discretization theory and practical, large-scale engineering analysis remains a critical pre-2026 challenge.
proposals.hypothesis: That the Variationally Mimetic Operator Network (VarMiON) framework, extended with the accurate reaction force/flux computation strategy for trimmed meshes, can be scaled to deliver efficient, stable, and high-fidelity solutions for the Reynolds-Averaged Navier-Stokes (RANS) equations on industrial-scale geometries using task-based parallel programming models.
proposals.method: We will develop a parallel implementation building on the VarMiON architecture, which learns stable mimetic operators from data. The core will integrate the recently published method for computing reaction forces and fluxes on trimmed, locally refined NURBS meshes to ensure conservation at domain boundaries and non-conforming interfaces. The framework will be implemented using the Charm++ runtime system for adaptive task-based parallelism, enabling dynamic load balancing on heterogeneous clusters. Scalability and accuracy will be validated on benchmark turbulent flow problems and a representative industrial case (e.g., a full aircraft configuration from the NASA High-Lift Common Research Model).
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver an open-source, scalable software prototype demonstrating that advanced mimetic discretizations can be practically applied to industrial CFD. It will provide a rigorous performance analysis of task-based parallelism for IGA-like methods on complex trimmed geometries. The results will establish a new pathway for integrating data-enhanced numerical operators into high-performance engineering simulation workflows.
proposals.novelty: The novel synthesis of the data-driven VarMiON framework with robust flux computation on trimmed CAD geometries and its implementation using adaptive task-based parallelism for industrial flow problems is unexplored. This moves beyond theoretical mimetic methods and proof-of-concept codes to address the concrete software engineering and scalability challenges of real-world simulation.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Inverse Isogeometric Analysis for Wall Boundary Condition Reconstruction in Turbulent Flows
proposals.motivation: In many engineering and biomedical applications, such as blood flow in arteries or aerodynamic design, the wall stresses or boundary conditions are critical but cannot be measured directly. Pre-2026, inferring these quantities from interior flow data is a challenging inverse problem, often ill-posed and sensitive to noise in the data. Existing methods struggle to enforce physical consistency and stability while handling complex geometries typical in real-world simulations.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a framework combining the geometric exactness of Isogeometric Analysis (IGA), the stability of Variational Multiscale Large Eddy Simulation (VMS-LES), and the structure-preserving properties of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VMONs) can robustly and accurately reconstruct unknown wall stresses or boundary conditions from sparse or noisy interior velocity data.
proposals.method: The method will leverage IGA on trimmed, locally refined meshes for geometric fidelity. The forward VMS-LES solver will be formulated within this IGA framework. The core inverse solver will be built upon the VMON architecture, treating the unknown boundary condition as a control variable. The network will be trained to minimize the discrepancy between the simulated and observed interior flow data, with the loss function incorporating the VMS-LES weak form as a physics-based constraint, ensuring mimetic properties like mass and momentum conservation. Optimization will use adjoint-based gradients computed on the refined IGA discretization.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-constrained computational framework for solving fluid inverse problems. It will advance the field by providing a stable, high-fidelity method for boundary condition reconstruction that is directly applicable to complex CAD geometries. The integration of VMONs with IGA and VMS-LES represents a significant step towards trustworthy digital twins for fluid systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of Hughes' core themes—IGA for geometry, VMS for turbulence, and mimetic operators for stability—into a single, principled inverse methodology. While VMONs have been proposed for forward problems, their application to ill-posed inverse problems in fluid mechanics, particularly for boundary condition identification on trimmed geometries, is unexplored.

proposals.title: Beyond the Finite Element Paradigm: A Systematic Investigation of Problem Domains Favoring Isogeometric and Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks
proposals.motivation: The finite element method (FEM) is the de facto standard for computational mechanics, but its dominance can obscure more efficient alternatives for specific problem classes. With the rise of isogeometric analysis (IGA) and novel operator learning frameworks, a principled comparison is needed to guide method selection. This work addresses a critical gap in numerical analysis by moving beyond anecdotal evidence to a systematic taxonomy of method superiority.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that for problems characterized by high-order continuity requirements, complex geometries with trimming, or solution manifolds with known underlying operator structure, IGA and Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiONs) will demonstrably outperform traditional FEM in accuracy, data efficiency, or computational cost per degree of freedom.
proposals.method: We will construct a benchmark suite of PDEs spanning solid/fluid mechanics and electromagnetics, with precise metrics for accuracy and cost. For high-order problems, we will employ IGA on trimmed, locally refined meshes, leveraging recent advances in reaction force computation. For problems with repetitive operator evaluation, we will design VarMiON architectures, using the 'input' and 'varmion' frameworks, whose structure is informed by the variational form of the differential operator. A key technical step is the generation of optimal quadrilateral layouts for IGA surfaces using equivalence classes of integral curves.
proposals.contribution: This research will produce a validated decision framework for numerical method selection in computational physics. It will provide concrete evidence and benchmarks demonstrating where abandoning the FEM paradigm yields significant advantage, thereby influencing future solver development. The work will also advance the practical application of VarMiONs and trimmed-IGA to challenging real-world geometries.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the systematic, head-to-head comparison driven by specific problem attributes rather than method promotion, and in the integration of cutting-edge geometric processing (trimming, layout generation) with the nascent paradigm of variationally mimetic neural operators.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for Nonlinear Structural Dynamics in Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: The simulation of nonlinear structural dynamics, involving large deformations and complex material laws, is critical for engineering applications like crashworthiness and seismic design. Traditional finite element methods can struggle with accuracy and stability in these regimes, while isogeometric analysis (IGA) offers superior geometric fidelity but requires robust time integration. Recent advances in Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiONs) provide a framework for learning solution operators of parametric PDEs with provable stability, but their application has been largely confined to linear problems.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the VarMiON architecture, grounded in variational mimetic principles, can be extended to learn stable and accurate solution operators for the nonlinear equations of structural dynamics, effectively capturing both geometric and material nonlinearities within an IGA framework.
proposals.method: We will extend the VarMiON framework to handle the nonlinear semi-discrete equations arising from IGA spatial discretization of dynamic problems. The network's architecture will be adapted to incorporate the history-dependent state variables essential for nonlinear material models (e.g., plasticity). Time integration will be embedded within the learned operator using a Newmark-beta or generalized-alpha method as a differentiable layer, ensuring energy-mimetic properties are preserved. The training data will be generated from high-fidelity IGA simulations of representative nonlinear problems on trimmed, locally refined meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will produce a new class of efficient, physics-aware neural operators for nonlinear dynamic simulation, directly applicable to complex CAD geometries. It will demonstrate how mimetic principles can ensure numerical stability in data-driven nonlinear mechanics, bridging the gap between traditional IGA and modern operator learning.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the VarMiON's variational structure with the geometric exactness of IGA and the challenges of nonlinear dynamics, creating a stable neural operator for a problem class where standard deep learning methods often fail to respect fundamental physical laws.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for Robust Partitioned Fluid-Structure Interaction
proposals.motivation: Partitioned fluid-structure interaction (FSI) simulations are essential for cardiovascular and aerospace applications but suffer from numerical instability and energy imbalance at the fluid-solid interface, especially with added-mass effects. Pre-2026 methods often rely on strong-coupling iterations or artificial damping to stabilize these simulations, which increases computational cost or reduces physical fidelity. A method that intrinsically enforces stability in a partitioned framework is a critical unmet need.
proposals.hypothesis: By embedding the Variationally Mimetic Operator Network (VarMiON) architecture within a partitioned FSI solver, we can construct interface flux and traction operators that preserve the dissipative structure of the coupled system, leading to unconditionally stable simulations without sub-iteration.
proposals.method: We will develop a two-field partitioned FSI framework where the fluid (modeled with isogeometric analysis/trimmed NURBS) and structure solvers exchange interface traction and velocity data. The core innovation is replacing conventional flux predictors with a VarMiON, trained on high-fidelity monolithic or strongly coupled snapshot data. This network, acting as a learned differential operator, will map the interface state history to a variationally consistent flux that mimics the energy-dissipative properties of the underlying continuum problem. The approach will be integrated with the concept of 'improved dissipation' from the seed idea, explicitly enforcing an energy inequality at the discrete interface level.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, stable partitioned FSI algorithm that significantly reduces computational cost by enabling stable weak coupling. It will provide a rigorous framework for incorporating physics-informed machine learning into multi-physics coupling, moving beyond purely data-driven surrogates. The resulting methodology will be demonstrated on benchmark problems like incompressible flow past a flexible cantilever and a simplified aortic valve model.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the VarMiON architecture—a structure-preserving neural operator—with the partitioned FSI problem to directly address its fundamental stability pathology, rather than treating symptoms. This creates a new class of 'mimetic coupling operators' that are non-obvious extensions of existing artificial compressibility or Robin-based methods.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic Operator Network Framework for Strongly Coupled Thermo-Mechanical Analysis in Isogeometric Analysis
proposals.motivation: Coupled thermal-mechanical problems, such as those in metal additive manufacturing and hypersonic structures, require robust, high-fidelity solvers that preserve fundamental conservation laws across physics interfaces. Pre-2026 isogeometric analysis (IGA) excels at single-physics simulation but faces challenges in multi-physics integration, particularly on complex trimmed and locally refined meshes, where traditional coupling strategies can violate variational consistency and degrade solution quality.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that embedding a variationally mimetic operator network (VarMiON) as the core integrator within a monolithic IGA framework will yield a structure-preserving method for coupled problems that ensures exact discrete conservation of energy and momentum across the thermal and mechanical domains, even on trimmed geometries.
proposals.method: The method will extend the VarMiON architecture, a neural operator trained to mimic variational structure, to act as a unified integrator for both the heat equation and the equations of elasticity. It will be embedded within a monolithic solution scheme, using IGA spline bases for geometry and field discretization. The framework will leverage recent advances in computing reaction forces on trimmed meshes to ensure accurate flux and traction recovery at the coupling interface. The training data will be generated from high-fidelity IGA solutions of coupled problems on parametrized geometries.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, physics-aware computational framework for tightly coupled thermo-mechanical simulation that is inherently conservative on complex CAD geometries. It will advance multi-physics IGA by providing a theoretically sound and numerically robust integration strategy, directly applicable to industrial design and analysis problems involving thermal stresses.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the synthesis of the VarMiON—a structure-preserving neural operator—with monolithic IGA for multi-physics, moving beyond single-physics applications. This creates a new class of solvers that combine the geometric exactness of IGA with the stability and conservation properties of mimetic methods, which is non-trivial for coupled systems on trimmed meshes.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic Operator Network for Kirchhoff-Love Shells with C0-Continuous Displacement Fields
proposals.motivation: Isogeometric analysis (IGA) provides a powerful framework for Kirchhoff-Love shell formulations, which require C1-continuous basis functions for the displacement field. Constructing globally C1-smooth spline spaces on complex, trimmed geometries is computationally expensive and algorithmically complex, hindering industrial adoption. This project aims to circumvent this bottleneck by developing a method that achieves the correct bending behavior using only C0-continuous elements, dramatically simplifying mesh generation and analysis.
proposals.hypothesis: The variational structure and higher-order differential operators of the Kirchhoff-Love shell problem can be enforced indirectly through a machine-learned mimetic operator, allowing a C0 displacement field to produce accurate bending solutions without explicit C1 continuity.
proposals.method: We will leverage the framework of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMiONs), which learn solution operators for parametric PDEs while preserving key variational structures. The network will be trained on a dataset of shell problems solved using high-fidelity IGA with C1 NURBS. The input to the VarMiON will be the C0 displacement field and geometric parameters from a standard finite element discretization. The network's learned operator will map these to the correct bending energy and stress resultants, effectively embedding the C1 requirement within its architecture. The method will be integrated with the core technologies of trimmed NURBS and locally refined meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, computationally efficient shell formulation that decouples geometric modeling from analysis continuity requirements. It will extend the applicability of isogeometric shell analysis to complex CAD models without costly global smoothness constructions. The project will also demonstrate a new application of physics-informed machine learning to overcome a fundamental constraint in computational mechanics.
proposals.novelty: While mixed and discrete Kirchhoff formulations exist, applying a VarMiON to implicitly enforce the C1 continuum condition within a C0 displacement space is a novel synthesis of IGA and operator learning. It directly targets the core computational expense of isogeometric shells using pre-2026 mimetic network architectures.

proposals.title: A Variationally Mimetic Staggered Scheme for Phase-Field Fracture: Inverting the Monolithic Operator for Robustness and Efficiency
proposals.motivation: Phase-field modeling of fracture couples a damage field with nonlinear mechanics, leading to highly nonlinear, ill-conditioned systems. Monolithic solution schemes, while robust, are computationally expensive and challenging to implement. Staggered (alternating minimization) schemes are popular for their simplicity and modularity but often suffer from convergence issues and lack of robustness, especially near critical loading states. A method that retains the stability of monolithic solvers with the efficiency of staggered schemes is needed.
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that the monolithic system operator for coupled phase-field mechanics can be inverted in a variationally consistent, staggered manner by constructing a preconditioner or surrogate solver based on the principles of Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks (VarMION). This will yield a robust, convergent staggered scheme with computational cost comparable to traditional staggered methods.
proposals.method: We will leverage Isogeometric Analysis (IGA) for spatial discretization, exploiting its high-order continuity for phase-field problems. The core innovation is to treat the fully coupled, monolithic Jacobian operator as a composite object. Using the VarMION framework, we will learn or construct a mimetic approximation of its inverse that respects the underlying variational structure. This approximated inverse will then be deployed within a staggered solution loop: solving for the displacement field with a frozen damage phase, then updating the damage field using the mimetic operator to implicitly account for the mechanical feedback, thus stabilizing the iteration. The approach will be tested on benchmark problems of brittle and quasi-brittle fracture.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, robust numerical scheme for phase-field fracture that bridges the gap between monolithic and staggered solvers. It will provide the computational community with an open-source implementation within an IGA framework, demonstrating significant gains in reliability and efficiency. The research will also extend the application of mimetic operator networks to a new class of coupled nonlinear problems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the direct application of the VarMION concept to *invert* the monolithic operator for use in a staggered scheme, a non-obvious synthesis of machine-learning-inspired numerical analysis with traditional operator splitting techniques for coupled physics.

proposals.title: Variationally Mimetic Operator Networks for Fluid-Shell Interaction: A Robust Framework for Hydraulic Fracture and Impact
proposals.motivation: The simulation of fluid-structure interaction (FSI) involving fracture in thin shells is critical for applications like hydraulic fracturing in energy geomechanics and impact/blast analysis in aerospace engineering. Pre-2026, robust coupling of high-fidelity fluid solvers with geometrically exact, fracture-capable shell models remains a significant computational challenge, especially on complex trimmed and locally refined meshes common in isogeometric analysis (IGA).
proposals.hypothesis: We hypothesize that a monolithic FSI framework, built upon variationally mimetic operator networks (VarMiON) for the fluid and isogeometric Kirchhoff-Love shells for the structure, will yield a stable, accurate, and geometrically flexible method for simulating fracture propagation driven by fluid pressure or impact.
proposals.method: We will extend the VarMiON architecture, a deep learning framework that mimics variational structure, to solve the incompressible Navier-Stokes equations on fluid domains deformed by the shell. The shell fracture model, utilizing IGA splines for high-order continuity and the trimmed element analysis for local refinement, will be coupled monolithically. The interface conditions will be enforced strongly using the non-uniform rational B-spline (NURBS) basis functions of the shell surface, and reaction forces/fluxes will be computed using the recently developed accurate strategy for trimmed meshes.
proposals.contribution: This work will deliver a novel, stable computational framework for FSI with fracture in thin-walled structures, directly applicable to hydraulic fracture and impact scenarios. It will demonstrate the first integration of a machine-learning-based fluid solver (VarMiON) with a high-fidelity IGA shell fracture model, advancing the field of predictive digital twins for complex multiphysics systems.
proposals.novelty: The novelty lies in the monolithic coupling of a variationally mimetic neural operator for fluids with an isogeometric shell fracture model, creating a new paradigm that combines the geometric exactness and local refinement of IGA with the solution efficiency of learned operators for coupled, dynamic fracture problems.

ideas.description: This redesigned approach embeds a partitioned, strongly-coupled implicit fluid-structure interaction solver within an immersed boundary framework to overcome the explicit time-step limitation. It uses a projection method or fixed-point iteration within each time step to ensure momentum conservation and exact satisfaction of the no-slip condition at the immersed boundary, even for very stiff materials. This allows for stable simulation of complex, rapidly moving boundaries—such as flapping wings or heart valves—with time steps comparable to those used for the pure fluid solver, dramatically improving computational efficiency and robustness.

ideas.description: This method enhances finite element approximations by systematically constructing and integrating problem-specific non-polynomial bubble functions. It first uses a coarse-grid error indicator to identify elements containing singularities or sharp layers. A local analytical solver then matches the asymptotic form of the expected solution feature to a parameterized library of non-polynomial functions (e.g., log(r), r^β, exponential boundary layers). These tailored bubbles are incorporated via an enriched local approximation space, ensuring the discrete solution captures the correct physics efficiently while maintaining numerical stability through a robust local variational implementation.

ideas.title: Non-Local Immersed Boundary Methods for Turbulent Flows with Complex Moving Boundaries
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes the immersed boundary (IB) method for handling complex moving geometries with a non-local paradigm for modeling subgrid-scale (SGS) effects. The core innovation is to formulate the IB's force distribution and the SGS stress closure not as purely local operations, but as integral operators that communicate information over a region influenced by the unresolved turbulence and the moving boundary's wake. This creates a coupled, non-local framework where the dynamics of the immersed boundary directly and physically modulate the surrounding turbulence model, and vice-versa, capturing essential bi-directional scale interactions. The result is a novel computational approach for predictive simulations of turbulent bio-fluid and engineering flows involving intricate motion, such as flapping wings or swimming organisms, where traditional local SGS models fail.

ideas.title: Benchmarking Subgrid Models via Geophysical Data Assimilation Techniques
ideas.description: This research idea adapts solution verification techniques from Numerical Weather Prediction (NWP) to systematically compare bubble function and stabilized finite element methods as subgrid-scale approximations in computational fluid dynamics. In NWP, diverse parameterizations for unresolved physical processes are rigorously benchmarked within data assimilation and ensemble forecast systems using observational data and high-resolution models as truth. This approach is adapted by treating high-fidelity Direct Numerical Simulation (DNS) or detailed experimental data as the reference 'truth.' The adapted research involves implementing both bubble and stabilized methods in a common solver framework and conducting ensemble simulations for a standardized set of benchmark flow problems. Their performance will be quantified by computing comprehensive error statistics—including L² and H¹ error norms, spectral analysis of resolved turbulence, and skill scores for drag or pressure—against the reference data, thereby objectively identifying the accuracy, robustness, and optimal application regimes for each subgrid-scale modeling approach.

ideas.title: Operator Learning for Physics- and Mesh-Adaptive Stability Parameter Fields
ideas.description: This project abandons the quest for a single analytical τ formula in favor of a learned, adaptive approach for complex problems. A hybrid system is developed that uses a theoretical branch to provide rigorous, but conservative, variational bounds on local time scales. This is coupled with a data-driven branch employing a neural operator trained to predict mesh-conditioning and local stability constraints from geometry and solution features. The final output is a robust, spatially varying τ field that ensures stability and accuracy for novel, non-uniform discretizations of complex geometries, effectively generalizing through learned adaptation rather than analytical derivation.

ideas.title: Uncertainty-Aware Intrinsic Time Scale Formulation for Complex Discretizations
ideas.description: This research direction synthesizes uncertainty quantification (UQ) with the generalization of the intrinsic time scale parameter τ. It aims to derive a probabilistic formulation for τ that accounts for geometric uncertainty, mesh non-uniformity, and parameter sensitivity in complex simulations. The core synergy is the development of a UQ-informed τ that provides not just a scalar value but a distribution, quantifying its reliability and its propagation's effect on solution stability and accuracy. This enables adaptive solvers to make robust time-step and discretization choices with known confidence levels, significantly enhancing predictive reliability in fields like fluid dynamics and material science where geometry and inputs are inherently uncertain.

ideas.title: Inverse Subgrid Modeling for Transient Problems on Evolving Domains
ideas.description: This research direction develops a framework to solve inverse problems for time-dependent PDEs where both the domain geometry and the fine-scale physics are unknown. It combines the derivation of data-informed, transient subgrid-scale models with the inversion of the Dirichlet-to-Neumann map to jointly reconstruct the evolving boundary and the missing microscale dynamics. The synergy allows for the solution of problems like tracking a moving corrosion front with unknown microscale reaction kinetics, or imaging a pulsating cell membrane while inferring subcellular transport processes, using only macroscopic boundary data. This advances beyond standalone model derivation or geometry inversion by enabling full-system identification in complex, multiscale environments.

ideas.title: Adaptive Mesh Refinement for Sub-Cellular Force Mapping
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution of Adaptive Mesh Refinement (AMR) from Computational Fluid Dynamics in Aerospace Engineering to the problem of resolving sub-cellular flow patterns and local shear stress gradients. In aerospace, AMR is used to dynamically increase grid resolution only in critical regions like wingtips and shock waves to capture steep gradients efficiently. The adapted research idea applies this principle by developing an algorithm that dynamically refines the computational mesh around cellular boundaries, organelles, or regions of high shear stress gradient within a biological flow model, while maintaining a coarser mesh in quiescent areas. This hybrid-scale approach enables the high-resolution mapping of forces at the cellular and sub-cellular level within the context of a larger tissue or organ-scale simulation, making detailed biomechanical analysis computationally feasible.

ideas.title: Vision-Augmented Dirichlet-to-Neumann Maps for Dynamic Boundary-Aware Subgrid Modeling
ideas.description: This research fuses the theoretical framework of unifying Dirichlet-to-Neumann (DtN) maps with subgrid scale models with the data-driven capability of computer vision for motion tracking. The core innovation is to use advanced optical flow algorithms to extract high-resolution, time-dependent wall motion from experimental or imaging data, which is then directly encoded as the dynamic boundary condition for the DtN operator. This dynamically informed DtN map provides a physically consistent exterior boundary condition that rigorously couples to an interior turbulence or multiphysics subgrid model, ensuring energy transfer and scale separation are respected. The result is a novel, boundary-aware simulation paradigm where real-world observed motion drives the computational model, enabling unprecedented accuracy for problems like cardiac flow, flapping flight, or fluid-structure interaction in complex geometries.

ideas.title: Apply the Mori-Zwanzig formalism from statistical mechanics to derive memory-dependent subgrid-scale models for transient PDEs
ideas.description: This research seed proposes to develop subgrid-scale model derivations for time-dependent partial differential equations by drawing an analogy from statistical mechanics. The analogous problem is the coarse-graining of high-dimensional Hamiltonian systems (e.g., molecular dynamics) into equations for a few macroscopic variables, which was solved using the Mori-Zwanzig projection operator formalism. This formalism yields a closed equation with a memory kernel, linking past states of the resolved variables to present forces. Adapting this solution, we treat the spatially discretized PDE system as the high-dimensional 'microscopic' system. Applying a projection onto the coarse-grid (resolved) degrees of freedom will formally derive a temporally non-local subgrid-scale model. The resulting model explicitly accounts for memory effects, moving beyond standard eddy-viscosity or similarity models, and provides a rigorous starting point for developing and assessing closure approximations for transient multiscale problems like turbulent flows or wave propagation in heterogeneous media.

ideas.title: A Methodology Transfer Framework: Using Approximate DtN Operator Techniques to Construct Localized Subgrid Closures
ideas.description: This project establishes a practical pipeline for translating techniques from the exterior boundary community to interior turbulence modeling, avoiding a vague theoretical unification. It systematically repurposes tools for building coarse-grained or learned DtN maps—such as rational approximations, neural operators, and boundary condition homogenization—to formulate novel, spatially compact subgrid-scale stress models. The core of the research is a direct, comparative performance assessment of these derived closures against classical LES models in well-defined, challenging computational fluid dynamics benchmarks. The outcome is a validated set of hybrid closure methodologies and a clear understanding of which approximation families transfer effectively, yielding concrete advances in simulation capability rather than abstract theory.

ideas.title: Couple fluid solver with an active fiber-reinforced composite model of the ventricle
ideas.description: This idea proposes to solve the challenge of coupling a fluid solver with active ventricular wall mechanics by drawing an analogy from aerospace composite modeling. In that field, the mechanical behavior of anisotropic, fiber-reinforced materials is successfully simulated by defining constitutive laws tied to local fiber orientation and coupling them with fluid dynamics solvers (e.g., FSI for wings). Adapted to cardiomechanics, the ventricular wall would be modeled as an active, fiber-reinforced composite. A finite element model of the myocardium would use a constitutive law that incorporates both the passive, anisotropic hyperelasticity derived from the collagen matrix and muscle fiber architecture, and an active tension component generated along the local fiber direction. This active tension is triggered by an electrophysiological model. The deformation of this active composite solid is then bidirectionally coupled with a CFD solver for ventricular blood flow, creating a comprehensive electromechanics-fluid interaction model.

ideas.title: Renormalization Group Framework for Systematic Accuracy Analysis of Subgrid Scale Approximations
ideas.description: This research direction develops a renormalization group (RG) based theoretical framework to systematically analyze and compare the accuracy of different subgrid scale approximations, such as bubble function and stabilized methods. The RG coarse-graining procedure provides a controlled, scale-by-scale decomposition of the solution, against which the error introduced by each approximation method can be quantified as a function of the resolved scale. This allows for a fundamental comparison not just of final solution accuracy, but of how each method distorts the scale-to-scale energy transfer and effective dynamics. The resulting framework offers a principled way to select, optimize, or hybridize subgrid models based on their RG flow behavior, rather than solely on numerical experiment.

ideas.title: Infer Arterial Wall Stiffness via Hemodynamic Pulse Waveform Inversion
ideas.description: This research adapts the solution to the geophysical inverse problem of seismic inversion, where subsurface material properties are estimated from observed seismic waves. The analogous field is Geophysics. The analogous solution involves using a forward model of wave propagation and iteratively inverting observed waveform data to estimate material parameters. Adapted to the original context, the idea is to invert a hemodynamic pulse wave propagation model to estimate the passive, distributed stiffness of an arterial wall segment. By treating observed blood pressure and flow waveforms as the 'seismic' data, an iterative inversion algorithm can be used to adjust the wall's constitutive parameters within a biomechanical model until the simulated pulse waveform matches the clinical observations, providing a non-invasive method for quantifying local arterial stiffness.

ideas.title: Derive intrinsic time scale via homogenization of wave propagation in composite media
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from wave mechanics in composite materials to derive an intrinsic time scale parameter τ. In acoustics, homogenization theory is used to derive effective wave speeds and time scales from the microstructure by solving a cell problem from a multiscale expansion. Here, we apply the same asymptotic homogenization method to a general dynamical system with fine-scale heterogeneity (e.g., in porous media, biological tissue, or composite materials). By introducing a fast spatial variable, performing the expansion, and solving the cell problem for the specific governing equations, we obtain a homogenized macroscopic equation. The parameter τ is then explicitly identified as the characteristic time scale emerging from the ratio of effective macroscopic parameters (like an effective diffusivity to the square of a characteristic macroscopic length), thereby rigorously linking the microstructural properties to the system's intrinsic temporal dynamics.

ideas.title: Model subgrid effects as non-local using integral transforms
ideas.description: This idea challenges the foundational assumption of locality in subgrid-scale modeling by drawing an analogy from continuum mechanics, specifically non-local integral models used in fracture mechanics. There, to avoid mesh-dependent results and capture long-range material interactions, stress is computed as a weighted spatial integral of the strain field over a finite region. Adapting this solution, we propose to model subgrid-scale stresses in turbulence simulations not as a purely local function of the resolved strain rate tensor, but as a non-local integral transform of the resolved velocity field over a domain surrounding each grid point. The kernel function would encode the non-local influence, theoretically allowing the model to represent spatial correlations, inverse energy transfer (backscatter), and scale interactions that are inherently non-local, moving beyond the limitations of conventional eddy-viscosity and similar local closures.

ideas.title: Immersed Boundary Methods for Urban Airflow Around Dynamic Structures
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from computational aeroacoustics, specifically the Ffowcs Williams-Hawkings acoustic analogy used for rotorcraft noise prediction, to the problem of simulating urban airflow around dynamic structures. The analogous solution efficiently handles complex moving surfaces like helicopter rotors by treating them as embedded discontinuities within a stationary fluid grid. The adapted research idea applies this principle to urban environmental fluid dynamics, proposing the use of an immersed boundary method to simulate atmospheric flow around moving architectural elements—such as adaptive building skins, opening bridges, or rotating towers—by embedding these dynamic boundaries within a fixed computational grid for the larger urban domain, thereby avoiding expensive mesh deformation and enabling studies of how moving urban forms affect pedestrian wind comfort, pollutant dispersion, and natural ventilation.

ideas.title: Quantifying the impact of subgrid-scale mechanical approximation fidelity on mechanobiological gene expression patterns in cardiac development
ideas.description: This research fuses numerical analysis and developmental mechanobiology by systematically comparing how bubble function and stabilized finite element methods, as subgrid-scale approximations, propagate error into the quantification of mechanical forces within a developing ventricle model. These quantified force fields, with their associated numerical error profiles, are then directly correlated with spatially resolved gene expression data (e.g., via RNA-seq from dissected regions or in situ hybridization). The core synergy is the ability to establish not just correlations between force and expression, but to determine which specific force components are most sensitive to numerical approximation and how this computational uncertainty impacts the identification of putative mechanosensitive genes and regulatory networks.

ideas.title: Learning Memory-Dependent Subgrid Closures for Transient Multiscale PDEs
ideas.description: This research direction develops a novel machine learning framework to derive and learn subgrid-scale closures specifically for time-dependent, multiscale partial differential equations. The core innovation is to architect neural networks—such as recurrent neural networks or neural ordinary differential equations—that learn closures which are non-Markovian, explicitly accounting for the history of resolved variables to model memory effects in the unresolved scales. This approach moves beyond static functional approximations to learn dynamic closure operators, enabling stable and accurate coarse-grained simulations of complex transient phenomena like turbulence, wave propagation, or reaction-diffusion systems over long time horizons. The result is a synergistic methodology that combines the data-driven power of machine learning with the rigorous mathematical structure of transient multiscale analysis.

ideas.title: Bayesian Calibration of Subgrid-Scale Models for Nonlinear PDEs with Uncertain Closure
ideas.description: This research synthesizes Bayesian inference and nonlinear subgrid-scale modeling to develop a novel computational framework. The core idea is to treat the critical closure parameter τ in nonlinear subgrid-scale models as a probabilistic quantity, enabling its Bayesian calibration against high-fidelity data or resolved simulations. This approach formally propagates uncertainty from the closure model into the final predictions for nonlinear PDEs like the Navier-Stokes or reaction-diffusion equations. The result is an adaptive, data-informed subgrid-scale model that provides not just a solution but a quantified confidence interval for simulations of turbulent or multiscale nonlinear systems, significantly enhancing reliability where traditional deterministic closures fail.

ideas.title: A Lagrangian-Eulerian Framework for Simulating Morphogenetic Force Evolution in Maturing Embryonic Tissues
ideas.description: This research direction combines the extended temporal scope of studying later embryonic maturation with the computational power of the immersed boundary (IB) method. The core innovation is to use the IB framework to explicitly simulate the evolving, active stress fields within growing and differentiating tissues as they interact with surrounding embryonic fluids and each other. This allows for the first predictive, mechanistic models of how force-generating processes like apical constriction, convergent extension, and differential growth collectively drive and are modulated by the large-scale, viscoelastic deformations characteristic of organogenesis and later morphogenesis. The synergy creates a closed-loop simulation environment where the history-dependent mechanical state of the tissue directly influences the generation of future active forces.

ideas.title: Renormalization-Group-Informed Machine Learning for Physically Constrained Subgrid Closure
ideas.description: This approach hybridizes analytical renormalization group (RG) methods with machine learning to overcome the fundamental closure problem in coarse-graining. The RG analysis is applied to the fine-grained equations to systematically derive the exact form of the coarse-grained equations, explicitly revealing the required non-local couplings and memory kernels that a practical model must incorporate. This structural output from RG then serves as a rigid physical template, constraining the architecture of a machine learning model (e.g., a neural network with specific non-local connections or a symbolic regression with a prescribed basis). The constrained model is trained on data from high-resolution simulations or experiments to learn the specific coefficients and residual functions, guaranteeing a subgrid model that is both physically consistent and computationally tractable.

ideas.title: Deriving τ via Graph Laplacian Spectral Methods from Network Science
ideas.description: This research adapts a solution from network science to derive a generalized analytical formula for the intrinsic time scale parameter τ in problems with complex, non-uniform meshes. In network science, the timescales of dynamical processes on irregular networks are determined by the eigenvalues of the graph Laplacian matrix, which encodes the network's connectivity. By analogously treating a computational mesh as a weighted graph (with vertices as nodes and mesh-element relationships as weighted edges), we can construct the mesh's graph Laplacian. The spectral properties of this operator—specifically its principal non-zero eigenvalue (the Fiedler value or algebraic connectivity)—yield a mesh-intrinsic time constant, τ ~ 1/λ₂. This provides a rigorous, geometry-agnostic definition of τ that depends solely on the topological and discrete metric properties of the mesh, generalizing the concept from uniform grids to arbitrarily complex discretizations.

It inverts a cardiac mechanics model using multi-phase image data to estimate regional passive myocardial stiffness, while explicitly modeling how registration errors from a single reference phase propagate through the cardiac cycle. By jointly solving for material properties and error propagation, the framework can separate true mechanical behavior from artifacts, yielding stiffness estimates that are corrected for the bias inherent in standard single-phase segmentation pipelines. This creates a more reliable foundation for patient-specific cardiac force quantification.

ideas.title: Renormalization Group-Informed Cellular Flow Models for Mechanobiology
ideas.description: This research direction develops a multiscale modeling framework that integrates renormalization group (RG) techniques with high-resolution cellular flow simulations. By applying RG analysis to the equations governing sub-cellular fluid-structure interactions, we derive scale-dependent parameters and universal functions that rigorously inform coarse-grained subgrid models for tissue-level simulations. This allows the tissue-scale model to not just approximate, but physically embody, the statistical mechanics of unresolved cellular-scale flow patterns and localized shear stress gradients. Consequently, the approach enables the first-principles study of how microscale mechanical heterogeneity influences emergent tissue phenotypes and vice versa, providing a closed-loop, physics-based tool for mechanobiology.

ideas.title: Multi-Phase Ensemble Registration for Robust Cardiac Strain Analysis
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that image registration for cardiac strain analysis must be anchored to a segmentation from a single cardiac phase. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where multi-phase seismic data joint inversion corrects for biases inherent to any single wave type, we adapt this to cardiac mechanics. The proposed solution is to perform independent segmentations at key, distinct phases of the cardiac cycle. Registration paths are initiated from each of these phases and then combined into an ensemble or fused displacement field. This method aims to prevent registration errors originating from a mis-segmented single phase from propagating and corrupting the entire strain and force quantification pipeline, thereby increasing the robustness and accuracy of the analysis.

ideas.title: Adapting Quantum Chemistry Basis Set Enrichment for Numerical Approximation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to a parallel problem in quantum chemistry, where standard Gaussian-type orbital basis sets are enriched with specialized functions (like Slater-type orbitals or explicitly correlated terms) to accurately capture challenging physical features like electron-nuclear cusps. The analogous solution involves selectively augmenting a primary basis with physically-motivated non-standard functions. Adapted to numerical approximation, this suggests employing enriched or non-polynomial bubble function spaces within methods like the Finite Element Method. The core research idea is to challenge the standard assumption of using purely polynomial approximation spaces by constructing hybrid spaces. These spaces would combine standard polynomial bubbles with locally defined, non-polynomial enrichment functions designed to capture known singularities, boundary layers, or other localized high-gradient features of the solution. This targeted enrichment aims to achieve significantly improved accuracy and convergence rates for problems with challenging local behavior, without resorting to globally fine meshes or uniformly high-order polynomials, thereby offering a more efficient and physically insightful discretization strategy.

ideas.title: Fusion of Direct Elastography and Hemodynamic Inversion for Resolving Passive Wall Stiffness
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the non-uniqueness of pure inverse modeling by integrating a direct, concurrent measurement of local tissue mechanical properties. High-frequency ultrasound shear wave elastography is used to acquire maps of wave speed (related to stiffness) in the vessel wall during the same observation period as motion and flow. A physics-informed Bayesian inversion model then uses these direct measurements as a spatially varying prior, constraining the solution space to physiologically plausible stiffness distributions. This fusion enables the robust estimation of truly passive wall stiffness by effectively isolating it from the confounding effects of active smooth muscle tone and autonomic regulation.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained, Adversarially-Trained Nonlinear Closure for Subgrid-Scale Modeling
ideas.description: This research develops a robust framework for subgrid-scale (SGS) modeling in nonlinear PDEs by directly addressing the complex closure problem through adversarial design and physical constraints. The core system uses a high-fidelity solver to generate training data across diverse nonlinear regimes, which informs a flexible SGS model architecture. This model is adversarially refined against a critic that identifies dynamical failures, ensuring stability in challenging flows. The final output is a generalizable, physics-constrained nonlinear closure model validated for accuracy and robustness beyond the training set, significantly advancing predictive capability for turbulent and multiscale nonlinear systems.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Hybrid Neural Closure with Stability Guarantees
ideas.description: This approach designs a hybrid subgrid closure that combines a simple, stable physical model (e.g., a Smagorinsky-type term) with a neural network correction. The neural network's architecture and training are rigorously constrained by embedded physical invariances (like Galilean invariance) and hard-coded conservation laws for key quantities (like energy). Training employs a composite loss function that penalizes violations of these principles, and the system is validated through long-term stability tests and a posteriori analysis of fully coupled simulations to ensure it does not induce numerical blow-up.

ideas.title: Invert wall motion causality: A fluid-structure interaction analogy from geotectonics
ideas.description: This research challenges the assumption that flow forces are the primary driver of wall motion in fluid systems. Drawing an analogy from geophysics, where the causality between mantle convection and plate motion was inverted (recognizing slab pull as a primary driver), we propose to invert causality in fluid-wall systems. The analogous solution involved treating the solid component (lithospheric plates) as active force generators that modify the fluid system (mantle flow). Adapting this, we will investigate if wall motion (from active biological contraction, engineered actuation, or elastic recoil) primarily drives flow patterns instead of being their consequence. This will be tested through experiments that independently control wall kinematics while measuring flow response, coupled with development of theoretical models where wall forces are independent variables in the fluid-structure interaction.

ideas.title: Sparse, Physics-Constrained Graph Networks for Hybrid Local/Non-Local Subgrid Modeling
ideas.description: This approach overcomes the intractability of fully non-local models by developing a hybrid framework that strategically blends local and non-local closures. A graph neural network architecture constructs a sparse, dynamic interaction graph between grid cells, where connections are formed based on learned, physics-informed criteria such as alignment with strain or vorticity fields. The model learns to apply intense, non-local corrections only where necessary (e.g., near shocks or turbulent fronts) while defaulting to efficient local operations elsewhere. This results in a subgrid model that captures essential non-local effects like backscatter and long-range stress correlations while maintaining computational scalability and physical interpretability for practical CFD simulations.

ideas.title: Concurrent Hybrid Atomistic-Continuum Solver with Adaptive Domain Placement
ideas.description: This system integrates continuum mechanics with atomistic simulations in a single, concurrent computational framework. It uses a dynamic error estimator within the continuum domain to automatically identify regions requiring atomistic resolution, such as crack tips, dislocation cores, or chemical reaction fronts. The solver then spawns and manages embedded atomistic domains at these locations, passing traction and displacement boundary conditions bi-directionally. This creates a self-consistent, first-principles-informed model that captures nano-scale heterogeneity without relying on approximate constitutive laws.

ideas.title: Learn Subgrid Closures via a Data-Assimilation-Informed Loss Function, Inspired by Geophysical Inverse Modeling
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution to geophysical inverse problems, specifically seismic full-waveform inversion, to the challenge of learning subgrid closures for turbulence or climate modeling. In the analogous field, subsurface properties are inferred by minimizing a loss that compares simulated to observed seismic data, constrained by the physics of wave propagation. Adapted to closure modeling, we propose training a neural network to represent the subgrid-scale term by minimizing a novel loss function. This loss function combines a traditional data-driven component (matching outputs from high-fidelity data) with a physics-informed component: the residual of the discretized coarse-grained equations, evaluated using available observational or partial high-resolution data. This approach leverages data assimilation concepts to 'invert' for the closure model, ensuring it not only fits data but also respects the underlying physical structure, leading to more robust and interpretable machine-learned closures.
