ideas.title: Use renormalization group for subgrid models, inspired by biological allometric scaling
ideas.description: Apply renormalization group techniques from statistical physics to coarse-graining, drawing an analogy from biological allometric scaling. In biology, scaling laws like Kleiber's law are derived from network principles and energy constraints, providing coarse-grained models that predict system behavior across scales without detailing every microscopic component. Adapt this solution by using the renormalization group to derive subgrid-scale models for turbulence. The approach enforces fundamental invariances and conservation laws across scales, leading to the derivation of scale-aware closure models. These models are defined by their renormalization group flow, offering a systematic, parameter-reduced alternative to empirically tuned subgrid models in computational fluid dynamics.

ideas.title: Variationally-constrained homogenization for physically-identifiable intrinsic time scales
ideas.description: This approach couples the mathematical derivation of an intrinsic time scale τ via asymptotic homogenization with a variational constraint that ties the parameter to measurable system dynamics. The homogenization procedure provides the functional form of τ in terms of microstructural parameters, while an embedded optimization loop ensures the resulting coarse-grained model best reproduces key observables, such as relaxation spectra or correlation decay, from high-fidelity data. This dual requirement guarantees that τ is not just a formal mathematical grouping but a physically identifiable quantity with a clear operational definition. The outcome is a robust, validated procedure for extracting intrinsic time scales from heterogeneous systems, directly applicable to model calibration and design.

It uses the Dirichlet-to-Neumann map as a forward model within a Hamiltonian Monte Carlo or variational inference framework, explicitly incorporating geometric and smoothness priors on the unknown boundary. The method outputs a posterior probability distribution over plausible domain shapes, directly quantifying reconstruction uncertainty. This provides robust, regularized solutions suitable for applications with noisy data, such as non-destructive testing or medical imaging.

ideas.description: This approach formally abandons the concept of a fixed cutoff scale. Instead, it employs a continuous wavelet spectrum to decompose the flow field, defining a scale-dependent nonlinear flux function that represents energy transfer across all scales. A neural operator, trained on direct numerical simulation or experimental data, learns this flux function under hard constraints derived from conservation laws. The resulting model dynamically adapts to the local scale content of the flow, providing a unified closure that is rigorously defined, calibratable, and valid for flows where traditional scale separation fails.

ideas.title: Homogenization-Informed Subgrid Closure for Nanoscale Material Dynamics
ideas.description: This research direction integrates the subgrid modeling of atomic-scale heterogeneity with the rigorous mathematical apparatus of asymptotic homogenization. The core innovation is to use homogenization theory on a representative atomic ensemble or defective microstructure not to produce a simple effective property, but to formally derive the governing equations for a subgrid field that captures nanoscale dynamics. This derived subgrid model includes an intrinsic time scale (τ) and coupling terms that are physically consistent with the underlying atomistics, moving beyond phenomenological calibration. The result is a predictive continuum-scale simulation capability that can model time-dependent processes like stress relaxation near grain boundaries, defect migration, or short-range ordering in complex alloys.

ideas.title: Subgrid Mechanogenomics: Modeling Atomic-Scale Cell-Cell Tension as the Primary Driver of Cardiac Trabeculation
ideas.description: This research direction fuses the biomechanical hypothesis of Idea A with the computational methodology of Idea B to create a 'mechanogenomic' subgrid model. We will develop a continuum model of the developing heart wall where the material properties and active stress generation at each macroscale point are governed by a subgrid model simulating the stochastic, force-sensitive binding and unbinding of adhesion proteins (like N-cadherin) at the atomic scale. This allows us to directly simulate how nanoscale heterogeneity in cell-cell adhesion complexes, influenced by genetic expression profiles, integrates into emergent tissue-scale tension fields. The core novel capability is to computationally isolate and test if these autonomously generated tension patterns are sufficient to drive the buckling and patterning events characteristic of trabeculation, independent of fluid shear stress.

ideas.title: Dual problem: forces to predict morphology via computational topology optimization

In engineering, topology optimization algorithms take load cases and design constraints as input to generate an optimal material layout. Here, we use spatiotemporal mechanical force data (the 'load cases') as input to a growth and remodeling model whose rules are formulated as a biological analog of the optimization algorithm. The model iteratively simulates bone deposition and resorption driven by local mechanical stimuli (e.g., strain energy density), starting from an initial geometry. This process converges on a predicted trabecular architecture that is optimally adapted, in a biological sense, to the given force history, thereby solving the dual problem of predicting morphology from forces.

ideas.description: This redesigned approach overcomes the limitations of scaling to later developmental stages by implementing an adaptive, multi-modal imaging and modeling pipeline. It dynamically selects and integrates the most suitable imaging technique (e.g., light-sheet, OCT, micro-CT) for different tissue depths and regions as opacity and size increase. The force inference framework is correspondingly modular, using a hierarchical model that applies high-resolution cellular-scale models where possible and coarser, continuum-based models for deeper or denser tissues. This allows for the continuous, validated mapping of mechanical force evolution from early organogenesis through to late-stage fetal maturation.

ideas.title: Adapting Seismic Tomography for Embryonic Force Maturation
ideas.description: This idea adapts a solution from geophysics to extend biomechanical analysis to later embryonic stages. In seismology, seismic tomography solves the inverse problem of mapping the Earth's evolving interior by analyzing waves from multiple earthquakes over time. Analogously, we propose to treat the embryo's own periodic mechanical activities (like contraction waves or large-scale tissue movements) as internal 'source events.' By applying computational inverse methods to the changing propagation patterns of these mechanical perturbations across later stages (e.g., organogenesis), we can reconstruct the spatiotemporal evolution of tissue material properties. This allows the study of force evolution not just as applied loads, but as a function of the maturing force-transmitting environment itself, providing a dynamic 3D map of mechanical maturation.

ideas.title: Renormalization-Group-Informed Machine Learning for Multi-Scale Force Prediction
ideas.description: This research direction integrates renormalization group (RG) theory with machine learning to develop a novel class of subgrid force models. Instead of training a standard ML model on high-resolution simulation data, the method trains a neural network to learn the RG flow equations governing how force interactions evolve under coarse-graining. The resulting model can take image data at any resolution and predict the corresponding effective forces, embedding the scale-invariance and universality principles of statistical physics directly into the ML architecture. This enables accurate, generalizable predictions across a continuum of scales, which is unattainable by either approach in isolation.

ideas.title: Homogenization-Informed Growth and Remodeling for Multiscale Trabecular Morphogenesis
ideas.description: This research direction integrates asymptotic homogenization directly into a dynamic growth and remodeling (G&R) model to predict trabeculation patterns. The homogenization theory is used to derive the intrinsic time scale parameter (τ) from the microscale cellular and matrix properties, making it a spatially and temporally evolving field within the G&R simulation. Consequently, the force-driven morphological predictions are no longer based on a single, calibrated τ, but on a mechanobiological feedback loop where the current microstructure dictates the rate of its own future adaptation. This enables the first-principles simulation of trabecular pattern formation, from a homogeneous initial state to a complex architecture, where the emergent time scales are a direct result of the interplay between global loading and local tissue properties.

ideas.title: Adapt Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) from fluid dynamics for robust cardiac wall motion tracking
ideas.description: This idea adapts the core methodology of Particle Image Velocimetry (PIV) from experimental fluid dynamics to the problem of cardiac wall motion tracking in echocardiography. In PIV, the motion of seeded particles in a fluid is tracked via cross-correlation of interrogation windows between laser-lit image pairs to generate a dense displacement field. The adapted solution treats natural acoustic speckle in ultrasound images as the analogous 'particles'. A multi-scale, window-based cross-correlation algorithm is applied to sequential echocardiographic frames to compute a robust, dense vector field of myocardial wall motion. This method is inherently more resilient to the noise and dropout artifacts common in ultrasound than standard variational optical flow techniques, providing a physics-inspired, quantitative approach to motion extraction.

ideas.title: Bayesian Calibration of τ using Climate Model Parameter Emulation
ideas.description: This idea adapts the Bayesian calibration methodology from climate science, where computationally expensive models with poorly constrained parameters are probabilistically calibrated using observational data. The analogous solution involves creating an efficient emulator for the complex model to enable feasible Bayesian inference. Adapted to the original context, we treat the τ parameter probabilistically by first constructing a surrogate model for the system of interest. We then apply Bayesian calibration methods: defining a prior for τ, establishing a likelihood function based on the discrepancy between emulator outputs and observed data, and using computational inference (like MCMC) to derive the full posterior distribution for τ. This approach formally quantifies the uncertainty in τ and allows for robust, uncertainty-aware predictions and decision-making.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained, Uncertainty-Aware ML for Robust Force Prediction from Real-World Images
ideas.description: This system trains a hybrid machine learning model on both simulated and limited real-world experimental data. The model architecture embeds fundamental physical constraints (e.g., conservation laws, symmetry) directly into the learning process via differentiable physics layers. An integrated uncertainty module quantifies epistemic (model) and aleatoric (data) uncertainty for each prediction, identifying when inputs are outside the model's reliable domain. This approach ensures robust, interpretable, and safe force predictions from diverse real-world images by preventing overconfident extrapolation from simulation data alone.

ideas.title: Decouple Hemodynamic and Cellular Force Contributions to Trabeculation Using a Multi-Mutant Computational-Experimental Pipeline
ideas.description: This project fuses the computational hemodynamic workflow with the core question of whether trabeculation requires forces beyond blood flow. We will apply the established pipeline to a panel of zebrafish mutants with distinct structural defects (e.g., silent heart, weak atrium, altered valve function) to generate mutant-specific hemodynamic predictions. Crucially, by comparing these predictions with high-resolution imaging of actual trabeculation outcomes and direct measurements of cardiomyocyte cell-cell tension in each mutant, we can computationally 'subtract' the hemodynamic contribution. This integrated approach will reveal which mutant phenotypes are rescued by restoring hemodynamics alone and which require correction of intrinsic cellular forces, definitively mapping the morphogenetic landscape.

ideas.title: Physics-Constrained Deep Learning for Robust Cardiac Motion Tracking in Echocardiography
ideas.description: This system combines the dense motion estimation of computer vision with a deep learning model trained on simulated and labeled echocardiogram data. The model architecture incorporates a recurrent neural network to enforce smooth temporal trajectories and is regularized by a loss term derived from simplified biomechanical principles of myocardial tissue. This hybrid approach ensures the extracted wall motion is not only data-driven but also physiologically plausible, significantly improving robustness in the presence of acoustic noise, dropout, and patient movement compared to pure optical flow methods.

ideas.title: Apply a Weather Forecasting Emulator Paradigm for Rapid Force Prediction from Images
ideas.description: This idea adapts the solution from climate and weather science, where ML emulators are trained on expensive simulation data to provide rapid forecasts. The analogous problem of slow, high-fidelity physics simulations for force prediction is solved by creating a machine learning-based emulator. We train a deep learning model (like a Vision Transformer or CNN) on a vast, pre-computed dataset from high-fidelity simulations, where inputs are image-based representations of material or structural states and outputs are the associated forces. This model learns the complex mapping from visual state to mechanical outcome, enabling it to act as a fast surrogate. When presented with new experimental or operational image data, the emulator can predict the corresponding force fields almost instantaneously, bypassing the computational cost of running a new simulation for each query.

ideas.title: Causal Perturbation Mapping of Flow-Driven Wall Motility
ideas.description: This research program directly tests the hypothesis that fluid dynamic forces are a primary driver, not a passive consequence, of wall motion in biological conduits. It overcomes the observational equivalence problem by employing precise, localized optogenetic activation of mural cells or targeted mechanical impulses to generate controlled flow perturbations, while simultaneously measuring resultant wall motion with microsecond resolution. The core analysis uses transfer entropy and Granger causality metrics on the perturbation-response data streams to establish temporal precedence and predictive power, definitively mapping causal flow-to-wall force pathways in developing and active microvascular networks.

ideas.title: Causally link specific force patterns to mechanotransductive signaling and gene expression in vivo
ideas.description: This study employs a closed-loop experimental system to move beyond correlation and establish causality between mechanical forces and gene expression in the developing heart. It combines tools for spatiotemporally precise mechanical perturbation (e.g., optogenetic actuators of cell contractility) with live imaging of rapid mechanosensitive biosensors and subsequent single-cell transcriptomic analysis. This approach allows for the direct experimental manipulation of force patterns within the intact embryonic ventricle while recording the immediate molecular response and definitive transcriptional outcome. The system is designed to dissect the causal chain from specific mechanical input, through key signaling pathways, to precise changes in gene expression programs.

ideas.title: Adapt Ecological Resilience Indicators as Model Uncertainty Diagnostics
ideas.description: This idea adapts uncertainty quantification methods from engineering to assess input data and model parameter sensitivity by drawing an analogy from ecosystem science. In that field, the resilience of an ecosystem to stressors is quantified using a suite of indicators (e.g., functional redundancy, response diversity, early warning signals) that diagnose its sensitivity and proximity to tipping points. The adapted solution is to create a parallel diagnostic framework for machine learning models. This involves developing a suite of computable 'model resilience indicators'—such as measuring prediction diversity under parameter perturbation, assessing functional redundancy in neural network layers, and detecting statistical early warning signals in training dynamics or loss landscapes. These indicators would collectively provide a multi-dimensional, interpretable profile of a model's robustness, specifically diagnosing its sensitivity to input noise and parameter variation, thereby offering a more holistic view of uncertainty sources than traditional single-metric approaches.

ideas.title: Prior-Robust Bayesian Calibration with Inferential Robustness Diagnostics for τ
ideas.description: This approach advances simple Bayesian calibration by systematically evaluating and mitigating sensitivity to prior specification for the τ parameter. It implements a computational workflow that fits the model across a designed ensemble of plausible prior families and hyperparameter settings, quantifying the variation in key posterior summaries. The system then employs Bayesian model averaging or stacking of predictive distributions to synthesize results, while diagnostics explicitly flag which inferences are robust. This yields a more objective and trustworthy uncertainty quantification for τ, clearly distinguishing solid evidence from modeling assumptions.

ideas.title: Assume trabeculation only needs hemodynamics: A morphogenesis analogy from plant root development
ideas.description: This idea challenges the assumption that hemodynamic forces are the primary driver of cardiac trabeculation by drawing an analogy from plant root hair development. In that field, the analogous problem of assuming external signals (nutrient gradients) as the primary morphogenetic driver was solved by discovering an internal, mechanically driven prepattern (localized cell wall softening). Adapting this solution, we propose that trabeculation is similarly initiated by a non-hemodynamic, internally generated mechanical prepattern—such as patterned cell-cell tension or differential adhesion within the cardiomyocyte layer—which establishes the sites for trabecular ridge formation. Hemodynamic forces would subsequently play a permissive or modulatory role, shaping and reinforcing these pre-patterned structures rather than instructing their initial emergence. The research would test this by genetically or pharmacologically manipulating cardiomyocyte cytoskeletal tension or adhesion in the absence of blood flow changes.

ideas.title: Decision-Aware Uncertainty Quantification with Adversarial Validation
ideas.description: This approach moves beyond passive uncertainty measurement to actively evaluate its practical implications. It embeds UQ within a decision framework that assesses how uncertainty propagates to risk in specific application contexts, such as clinical or operational decisions. The system employs adversarial examples and synthetic edge cases to stress-test the calibration and robustness of the predicted uncertainty intervals. Finally, it outputs validated, pragmatically useful confidence indicators that specify whether the uncertainty is low enough to support a given high-stakes action or necessitates gathering more information.
